vector_chunks.text:
ge pattern)

- **Tissue Detachment\
 > **

 - If \>5 tissues begin detaching, notify Ada and make a plan.

 - If \>15 tissues detach, place spacers immediately.

 - Otherwise:

 1. No spacers in cycles 1-3 unless detachment occurs.

 2. 'If tissues are detaching place spacers in **ALL** cycles.

- **Slide Management\
 > **

 - Ada will provide a guide on when to re-scan and when not to.

 - Plan and divide work into batches.

 - Follow standard naming conventions and ordered scanning
 > practices.

> **[Post-Experimental Practices]{.underline}**

- **Daily Backups\
 > **

 - Data must be backed up in at least two locations.

- **Book Workstations in Advance\
 > **

 - Ensure sufficient computational resources are available for
 > analysis.

- **Update Code in GitHub\
 > **

 - Keep all experimental and analysis code updated to prevent loss.

- **Review and Analyze Results\
 > **

 - Ensure proper discussion and documentation of experiment
 > outcomes for future planning.

**[Scanning quality]{.underline}**

You may look for the following:

- Antibodies showing the expected pattern -\>

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Some antibodies like CCNE, pRPA or p21 are not expected to be seen
 > in all samples, if they work in some of them and do not show
 > signal in others it is normal and expectable

- If not, we should internally check if we continue scanning or if we

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.md
vector_chunks.text:
in all samples, if they work in some of them and do not show
 > signal in others it is normal and expectable

- If not, we should internally check if we continue scanning or if we
 > repeat the staining

 - Key antibodies affected -\> repeating staining

 - 5th/6th cycles antibodies affected -\> continue scanning,
 > replanning experiment after that round of staining

 - If p27 does not work -\> we continue

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- Intense antibody aggregates

 - If they are tiny and all around, this counts as the antibody not
 > working.

 - If there big but constrained to a small area (\<5-10% of the
 > tissue), we continue

 - The aggregate is outside the tissue, or above fat areas, we
 > continue

- Blurry antibody aggregates

 - They are round and have lower or similar signal than
 > [[positives. If]{.underline}](http://positives.if) we have a
 > CD4, CD45, CD3D, CD8a, CD20, FOXP3 antibody, we do not care.
 > If it is functional we can correct but it is not ideal. If it
 > covers \<10% of the tissue, continue, otherwise wash, re-mount
 > and scan again.

- Computer-generated artifacts (we will ALWAYS ignore these)

 - If you see tiles missing, squared tiles suddenly appearing etc,
 > just ignore and continue

- Hairs, fibers, etc. If they do not cover tumor area, continue. If
 > they cover tumor area, remount and re-scan

- Blurry tissue - redo the focus points and re-scan. Changing slightly
 > the DAPI intensity might help.DAPI is not inside the analysis and

Small aggregates (re-stain) Big aggregates (follow)

**IF Staining Protocol - Summary for Cyclical Scanning Quality Control**

1. **Is antibody showing expected pattern?\
 > **

 - **YES**: Continue scanning.

 - **NO**: Is the antibody critical (tumor, cellcycle functional,
 > DNA repair)?

 - **YES**: Repeat staining.

 - **NO**: Continue scanning.

2. **After 5th/6th cycle, is antibody showing expected pattern?\
 > **

 - **YES**: Continue scanning & Replan after 6th cycle.

 - **NO**:Co ntinue scanning & Replan after 6th cycle

3. **Are there intense antibody aggregates?\
 > **

 - **YES**:

 - **Aggregates \< 5-10% of tissue or outside tissue/fat**:

> Continue scanning.

- **Small and dispersed aggregates \>5-10% tissue**

> Go to 1)NO and pick your solution.

- **Large aggregates**: Remount and re-scan.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_10-Expansion_tCycIF/Adas guidelines for scanning.md
vector_chunks.text:
utside tissue/fat**:

> Continue scanning.

- **Small and dispersed aggregates \>5-10% tissue**

> Go to 1)NO and pick your solution.

- **Large aggregates**: Remount and re-scan.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **NO**: Continue scanning.

4. **Are there blurry antibody aggregates?\
 > **

 - **YES**:

 - **\< 10% of tissue and filterable antibodies**

 - **(e.g., CD4, CD45, CD8,CD20,etc - NON-FUNCTIONAL)**:

> Continue scanning.

- **\> 10% or non-functional aggregates**: Wash, re-mount, and
 > re-scan.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **NO**: Continue scanning.

5. **Are there computer-generated artifacts (missing tiles, unexpected
 > Appearances)?\
 > **

 - **YES/NO**: Ignore and continue scanning.\
 > .

6. **Are there hairs, fibers, etc.?\
 > **

 - **YES**:

 - **Covering tumor area**: Remount and re-scan.

 - **Not covering tumor area**: Continue scanning.

 - **NO**: Continue scanning.

7. **Is tissue blurry?\
 > **

 - **YES**: Re-do focus points and re-scan (adjust DAPI intensity
 > if necessary).

 - **NO**: Continue scanning.

vector_chunks.text:
\# Base paths

TARGET_BASE=\"/Volumes/h30492/farkkilab2/Backup/4_CellCycle/2025_4_10_Expansion/raw\"

SOURCE_BASE=\"/Volumes/h30492/farkkilab2/Backup/4_10_Expansion/cycle0_main\"

\# Loop through all cycle0 folders

for src_folder in \"\$SOURCE_BASE\"/\*; do

if \[ -d \"\$src_folder\" \]; then

\# Extract sample name (like S005_iOme)

folder_name=\$(basename \"\$src_folder\")

sample=\$(echo \"\$folder_name\" \| sed -E
\'s/\^4\_(\[\^\_\]+\_\[\^\_\]+)\_.\*\$/\\1/\')

\# Corresponding target folder

target_folder=\"\$TARGET_BASE/\$sample\"

if \[ -d \"\$target_folder\" \]; then

\# Copy all .rcpnl files

cp -v \"\$src_folder\"/\*.rcpnl \"\$target_folder\"/

else

echo \"Warning: target folder \$target_folder not found, skipping.\"

fi

fi

done

vector_chunks.text:
CHOSEN ATTEMPT

-7 GOOD CLUSTERS
- MPM2 AND GEMININ HAVE NOT BEEN UTILIZED. THEIR DISTRIBUTIONS OVERLAPPED WITH THOSE SHOWN IN ph3/PRB AND THE NOISE INTRODUCED BY THESE CHANNELS (NOT SO WIDE DYNAMIC RANGE) WAS CREATING CHALLENGES WITH THE CLUSERING
- Distinct numbers of clusters have been tried 5-7, selecting 7 because of the biological meaning behind the shown clusters (corresponding to cell cycle phases and including an interesting CCNE enriched cluster that was not included with 6).

vector_chunks.text:
All% + specific % of intra-tumor cell cycle phase

Cluster 5 (high proliferation) has high correlation with CD4,CD8 T cells and B cells

Cluster 0 (G1) has high correlation with CD163+ macrophages and other immune cells

Cluster 6 (CCNE) is correlated with higher DC

Cluster 1 (arrested) is correlated with higher % of CD163+ DC, CD163- Mph, CD4 T cells

Intratumor cell cycle %

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/2.Clustering_attempts/1.8.24-1.11.24- PostEACR_clustering_cc_in_cells_attempts.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
All% + specific % of intra-tumor cell cycle phase

Intratumor cell cycle %

Cell cycle % within tumor cells belonging to:
0 (G1) and 5 (M) seem to be correlated with higher immune cell %
Moderate correlations are noted in;
6(CCNE) is correlated with higher DC %
3 (S-like) is also correlated with higher DC, CD8 T cell and B cell abundance (%)
2 (G2-like) and 4 (Dormant-like) are negatively correlated with immune cell%
-Clusters 2 and 6 are correlated with a higher overall tumor %, together with a lower stromal%

Overall, highest value correlations are seen in clusters 2, 5 and 6

vector_chunks.text:
Distance

Tumor 0 (G1) and 6 (CCNE) seem to be distant to the rest of the cell cycle tumor cells. Interestingly, 0 is close to myeloid cells


3 and 5 clusters seem to be very close distance-wise, which suggests the presence high-proliferation localized areas

Stromal cells are closer to 0,1,3,5 clusters, whilst 2 and 4 (unknown groups, poorly correlated in abundance with cycling phases) and 6, are more distant to the stroma. 2, 4 and 6 are also more distant to CD8 and CD4 and macrophages

Tumor 0 (G1) and 6 (CCNE) seem to be distant to the rest of the cell cycle tumor cells

3 and 5 clusters seem to be very close distance-wise

vector_chunks.text:
Scientific questions

How can we better classify these “arrested” phenotypes?
The preliminary analysis shows how the number of cells in these phases varies across samples, I would like to subcategorize patients based on those (eg S-phase enriched, M-enriched). CCNE1 rich-patterned patients seemed to have increased S-phase cells, which correlates with their biology. Maybe this strategy could also help stratifying the patients.
This data might be useful to create a replication stress score, and to also address checkpoint recovery

vector_chunks.text:
Considerations for metaclustering
•Fluorescence Variability : Differences between samples complicate normalization and cross -sample comparisons .
•Unknown Cluster Count : The number of clusters is not predefined , and some metaclusters may be unique to specific samples .
•Cell cycle aberrancies : Tumor s’ cell cycle is aberrant in a unique way, making the co-expression of certain markers possible
•Validation Requirement : Image -based validation is essential to confirm accuracy and biological relevance .

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/2.Clustering_attempts/27.02.25CellCyclemetaclusters_w_PRIMUS-reportforAnniina.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Current clustering strategies (II) – PRIMUS
PRIMUS is an expectation maximization -based tool
Expectation (E -step) -Guess the hidden info
Based on the current best guess, estimate the 
probability that each person belongs to each 
group.
Maximization (M -step) -Improve the guess. 
Update your guesses using the probabilities from 
the E -step to make them more accurate.Expectation Maximization

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/2.Clustering_attempts/27.02.25CellCyclemetaclusters_w_PRIMUS-reportforAnniina.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Current clustering strategies (II) – PRIMUS
tCycIF 
matrix
imageIDs
Vector of 1s
Expectation (E-Step ) – Estimate Hidden Variables
•For each marker in each cell, the model estimates how much of the observed count comes from nuisance effects and how much 
from biological clusters .
Maximization (M-Step ) – Update Estimates
•Using the probabilities from the E-step, refine the estimates of:
•The nuisance expression centroids 
•The latent phenotypic expression centroids
•The cluster memberships
Repeat Until Convergence
•The algorithm continues updating parameters until changes become negligible

vector_chunks.text:
Which are our controls ?
The last proteopicking experiment failed
•Results are disappointing , with weak signals barely distinguishable from background noise . Scrapes worked
•Unexpected Findings : An empty slide had more protein IDs than some actual samples , raising concerns about 
sample mix-up or excessive background measurement . 
•Experiment Setup : Used an LC column and method for single -cell proteomics , expected to slightly improve 
performance . Instrument performance was verified and deemed not responsible for low IDs.
•Potential Issues : Sample loss (e.g., retention in tubes ) or degradation , possibly worsened by prolonged storage .
•Sample Collection Concerns : Otto asks if there were significant differences in sample collection that could have 
affected results.Lina says she was just more careful with the process .
•Less Polymer /Keratin Interference : A positive aspect , but possibly at the cost of losing signals from important 
proteins .
•Next Steps : Otto suggests discussing the findings over a Zoom call.

vector_chunks.text:
# **Research Progress Report Template**

**Project Title:** Profiling of Spatial Tumor Microenvironment
Architecture in Highly Multiplexed Images of High-Grade Serous Ovarian
Carcinoma**\
Student Name(s):** Alva Grönholm**\
Supervisor:** Ada Junquera**\
Start Date:** August 2024**\
Expected Completion Date:** March 2025**\
GitHub Repository (if applicable):** [[Github
repository]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/cellcycle/tree/a002c6ac5d8e862e5732debd246d446fb35d09ff/Alvas%20MSc)\
**Shared Folder Link:**
[[3.Alvas_MSc]{.underline}](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1WzM_kk_qpxT0MN6O8Y_wAyvixd-KCqSZ?usp=drive_link)

## **Project Overview**

**Brief Description of Research Goals:\
**Refine image-derived quantified data through data normalization and
assignment of precise phenotype labels to the cells of the tumor
microenvironment (TME). Explore the composition of the TME by analyzing
the abundance of phenotypes across the cohort. Compare two different
spatial analysis methods---spatial count and latent Dirichlet allocation
(LDA)---using different parameters (radius: 13, 25, 35 microns) and
assessing their suitability for various types of analysis. Determine
which approach is better suited for specific analytical goals and
compare the results between distinct clinical groups to explore
potential differences and trends.

on the 33rd percentile.

### **Step 2: Spatial analysis - computing spatial count and LDA**

- **Description:** Compute spatial count and LDA and cluster the
 > results into RCNs. Spatial count and LDA are computed using the
 > following radii: 13 microns (40 px), 25 microns (77 px), and 35
 > microns (108 px). The results are clustered using KMeans
 > clustering, with the optimal number of clusters (k) determined
 > using elbow curves and the *KneeLocator* method from the
 > *kneelocator* package. Annotation of RCNs based on barplots and
 > image verification.

- **Expected Completion Date:** February 2025

Multi-omics profiling of cell -cycle spatial niches in 
high -grade serous carcinoma 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Genetics and Molecular Biosciences, 
Molecular and Analytical Health Biosciences , 
 Master's thesis 
 
Author: 
Nika Mikhailava 
 
Supervisors: 
Anniina Färkkilä 
Ada Junquera Mencía 
 
05.2026 
Helsinki

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Faculty: Faculty of Biological and Environmental Sciences 
Degree programme: Genetics and Molecular Biosciences 
Study track: Molecular and Analytical Health Biosciences 
Author: Nika Mikhailava 
Title: Multi-omics profiling of cell -cycle spatial niches in high -grade serous carcinoma 
Level: Master’s thesis 
Month and year : 05.2026 
Number of pages: 60 
Keyword s: ovarian cancer, high -grade serous carcinoma, tumor microenvironment, t-CyCIF, 
molecular profiling, cell cycle, spatial niche 
Supervisor s: Anniina Färkkilä, Ada Junquera Menc ía 
Where deposited: HELDA, Helsinki University Open Repository 
Additional information: 
Abstract: 
High -grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the deadliest gynecological malignancy , typic ally detected at 
advanced stages. The standard of care in more than 50% of cases comprises neoadjuvant chemotherapy 
(NACT), followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS) . HGSC is characterized by initial high 
chemotherapy response rates, yet 80% of patients ultimately relapse. While previous research has 
focused on “persister" cells - drug-tolerant tumor subpopulations that drive relapse. W e suggest that 
these do not capture the full spectrum of cells driving disease recurrence. We propose the term 
"subsister" cells to describe the broader , more heterogeneous population of tumor cells that survive first -

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ll spectrum of cells driving disease recurrence. We propose the term 
"subsister" cells to describe the broader , more heterogeneous population of tumor cells that survive first -
line chemotherapy , NACT , and retain the potential to fuel relapse with di fferent scales of aggressiveness 
and therapeutic targeting . 
We hypothesize that a critical hallmark of “subsist er” cells is dysregulation of the cell cycle . Previous 
research in our laboratory identified a specific highly proliferative patient archetype characterized by 
the prevalence of recurrent cellular neighbourhoods dominat ed by highly proliferative niches co-
expressing Ki67 and pRb proteins in IDS samples . Because the prevalence of these niches correlates 
with a short platinum -free interval (PFI) , we hypothesize that these niches represent an aggressive , 
chemotherapy -subsisting population. 
This study aimed to uncover the molecular features of “subsister” highly proliferative niches in IDS 
HGSC samples. To comprehensively evaluate these microenvironments , we employed a stepwise spatial 
multi -omics approach. First, we conducted a large -scale spatial proteomics experiment using tissue -
based cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CyCIF) to precisely characterize the physical location, cellula r 
composition , and prevalence of those niches across the patient cohort. This extensive protein -level

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
cence (t -CyCIF) to precisely characterize the physical location, cellula r 
composition , and prevalence of those niches across the patient cohort. This extensive protein -level 
mapping was subsequently integrated with spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiling 
(DSP) ) to explore the underlying transcriptomic mechanisms within those niches. 
Spatial transcriptomics analysis revealed that highly proliferative niches possess a characteristic 
transcriptional program . As expected, these niches demonstrated coherent upregulation of pathways 
governing active cell proliferation and altered cellular energetics. Beyond these expected findings, 
functional enrichment (single -sample gene set enrichment analysis and gene set enrichment an alysis ) 
highli ghted distinct survival programs utilized by those niches . Specifically, significant upregulation of 
DNA repair mechanisms, DNA synthesis, and sumoylation of DNA replication proteins reveals a 
specialized program to manage replicative stress introduced with rapid division. Simultaneously, t his 
was coupled with a metabolic shift toward the Glycine Metabolic Process , supporting the biosynthetic 
demands for nucleotide synthesis and antioxidant defense . 
Reflecting these pathway -level adaptations, differential gene expression (DGE) analysis highlighted 
specific drivers of this phenotype.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
otide synthesis and antioxidant defense . 
Reflecting these pathway -level adaptations, differential gene expression (DGE) analysis highlighted 
specific drivers of this phenotype. FGD5 and SOX13 emerged as the most significantly upregulated 
marker s in these niches, suggesting critical roles in maintaining "stem -like" plasticity and structural

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
integrity . Conversely, the prominent downregulation of CCND1 in these active niches aligns with our 
previous findings , associating CCND1 enrichment with a better prognosis. 
Together, these spatially resolved transcriptomics signatures provide critical insights into the HGSC 
"subsister " cell landscape . Characterizing these transcriptomic mechanisms may ultimately assist in the 
design of novel treatment strategi es that effectively target these aggressive niches and prevent disease 
relapse .

vector_chunks.text:
9 
 
 1 Introduction 
High -grade serous carcinoma ( HGSC ) is the most common and lethal subtype of 
ovarian cancer. The standard of care includes debulking surgery and platinum -based 
chemotherapy. While most patients initial ly respon d to the treatment, more than 75% 
of patients experience relapse . At least 50% of the patients receive the neoadjuant 
chemotherapy ( NACT ) as a first treatment , followed by interval debulking surgery 
(IDS) , to remov e the remaining tumor masses . The cells that survive the first line of 
chemotherapy are what we have named “ subsister ” cells . We hypothesize that these 
cells are driving the relapse and contributing to treatment resistance formation. 
Recent advances in spatially resolved imaging have enabled the characterization of 
tumor heterogeneity beyond genomic alterations, highlighting the importance of 
phenotypic tumor states and their spatial organization. Because the efficacy of most 
cytotoxic chemotherap ies is inherently tied to disruption of the cell cycle, the ability of 
certain tumor populations to bypass the G1/S, G2/M, and spindle assembly 
checkpoints is a primary driver of treatment failure . In this context, multiplexed 
imaging approache s such as tissue -based cyclic immunofluorescence ( t-CyCIF ) allow 
high -dimensional protein profiling across tissue sections at single -cell resolution. By

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
imaging approache s such as tissue -based cyclic immunofluorescence ( t-CyCIF ) allow 
high -dimensional protein profiling across tissue sections at single -cell resolution. By 
mapping the spatial distribution of key cell cycle regulators, this methodology provides 
a unique insight into functional tumor states and their spatial organization that 
govern s chemotherapy resistance and facilitate s the formation of “subsister” niches. 
Using t -CyCIF, our recent exploration identified discrete HGSC patient archetypes 
based on common patterns of their “subsister” cell populations (Fig. 1). These included 
a highly proliferative, C yclin D1 (CCND1) -enriched archetype, and a heterogeneous 
archetype exhibiting mixed phenotypic features. Notably, the highly proliferative 
archetype was associated with poorer platinum -free interval (PFI) and was 
independent of genetic alterations. This archetype was enriched in recurrent cellular 
neighbourhoods ric h in mitotically active tumor pH3+ cells and proliferative Ki67+ 
pRb+ cells. These findings suggest that “subsister” -based patient archetypes are 
clinically relevant, making it relevant to study their survival mechanisms.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
10 
 
 
Figure 1. Identification of HGSC patient archetypes through t -CyCIF imaging. Spatial protein expression 
patterns reveal three dis crete phenotypic archetypes: 1) Highly proliferative archetype, 2) Cyclin D1 
archetype, 3) Heterogeneous archetype . 
We hypothesize that these Ki67 ⁺pRb ⁺ highly proliferative “subsister” niches represent 
aggressive "subsister" cells. These cells possess phenotypic variations enabling them to 
survive chemotherapy and quickly adapt to drive the relapse. The transcriptional 
programs underlying these highly proliferative “subsister” niches re main poorly 
understood. It is unclear which gene expression signatures and biological pathways 
allow these highly proliferative regions to subsist under the selective pressure of 
treatment . Furthermore, it remains to be determined how these mechanisms relate to 
the observed phenotypic states, and whether they are uniquely patient -specific or 
broadly conserved across patients.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
11 
 
 2 Background 
2.1 Ovarian cancer: epidemiology, risk factors, and subtypes 
Ovarian cancer (OC) is recognised as the deadliest gynecological malignanc y, affecting 
approximately 200,000 -300,000 women annually (M. Wang et al., 2024). Globally , 
the incidence and mortality rates for ovarian cancer are 6.6 and 4.2 per 100.000 of 
women , respectively (Caruso et al., 2025). It is the 18th most common cancer 
worldwide and the 8th most common cancer among wome n. The median diagnosis age 
is 63 years, and the 5 -year survival is less than 50% (Caruso et al., 2025) . 
Ovarian cancer has 5 histo -molecular subtypes: HGSC, low -grade serous carcinoma 
(LGSC), mucinous carcinoma (MC), endometroid carcinoma (EC) , and clear cell 
carcinoma (CCC) (Prat, 2012) . Geographically, serous and mucinous carcinomas are 
more prevalent in high -income countries, while endometroid and clear -cell 
carcinomas in low -income countries, particularly in Africa and Eastern Asia (M. Wang 
et al., 2024) . Serous carcinomas appear as the most prevalent subtype of ovarian 
cancer, accounting for approximately 43% of all disease cases (M. Wang et al., 2024) . 
HGSC is the most aggressive and recurrent subtype of ovarian cancer (Govindarajan et 
al., 2020) . 
Common risk factors for ovarian cancer include older age, family history of breast or

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
the most aggressive and recurrent subtype of ovarian cancer (Govindarajan et 
al., 2020) . 
Common risk factors for ovarian cancer include older age, family history of breast or 
ovarian cancer, endometriosis, nulliparity , and infertility (Caruso et al., 2025) . 
Subtype -specific factors include menopausal hormonal therapy, increasing the risk for 
serous carcinomas (M. Wang et al., 2024) . Hereditary factors are associated with 25% 
of ovarian cancer cases (Caruso et al., 2025) . Factors pro tecting against ovarian cancer 
are multiparity, breastfeeding , and oral contraceptive use (M. Wang et al., 2024) . 
2.2 HGSC diagnosis and treatment 
Lack of symptoms in the early stages of HGSC poses a critical challenge for its clinical 
management. Consequently, the disease is mostly diagnosed at advanced stages. When 
present, the symptoms are often non -specific ( such as abdominal pain, bloating, and 
frequent urination) (Caruso et al., 2025) . 
Current screening methods for ovarian cancer are limited and include blood -derived 
carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125) and transvaginal sonography (M. Wang et al., 
2024) . The standard treatment regimen comprises cytoreductive (debulking) surgery

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
12 
 
 and platinum -based chemotherapy treatment. The specific treatment strategy depends 
on the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) stage. For 
patients with early -stage disease (FIGO stages I or II), the disease is managed with 
surgery a nd adjuvant chemotherapy. In advanced -stage cases of HGSC (FIGO stages 
III-IV), patients are treated with NACT followed by IDS. NACT typically consists of 3 
to 4 cycles of combination therapy using a platinum agent and a taxane. After NACT 
reduces the tumo r burden, patients undergo IDS – a surgery aimed at removing as 
much of the remaining macroscopic tumor as possible before completing the last cycles 
of chemotherapy. 
Following initial treatment, most patients achieve clinical remission. However, the risk 
of relapse remains high. Approximately 80% of patients diagnosed with advanced -
stage cancer and 20% of patients with early -stage cancer relapse. Prognosis is heavily 
influenced by the molecular characteristics of the tum or. For instance, germline and 
somatic homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) mutations in HGSC are 
associated with better prognosis (Pennington et al., 2014) . Tumors with HRD, often 
due to BRCA1/2 mutations, respond better to platinum -based chemotherapy and Poly 
(ADP -ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors. Conversely, tumors that are homologous

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
HRD, often 
due to BRCA1/2 mutations, respond better to platinum -based chemotherapy and Poly 
(ADP -ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors. Conversely, tumors that are homologous 
recombination proficient (HRP) are correlated with shorter progressio n-free survival 
and overall poorer disease outcomes. 
For patients who experience a disease recurrence, the timing of the relapse dictates the 
subsequent clinical approach. Those patients who respond to chemotherapy and 
relapse more than 6 months after the treatment are considered platinum -sensitive and 
are likely to respond to subsequent platin um-based treatments (Miras et al., 2024) . In 
contrast, patients who relapse within 6 months after chemotherapy are considered 
platinum -resistant and have a worse prognosis. 
To combat these recurrence rates and improve overall treatment efficacy, targeted 
therapies have become increasingly integral. Some of them are widely applied as 
maintenance therapies following platinum -based regimens (L. Wang et al., 2025) . 
Among these, PARP inhibitors are the most impactful of targeted therapies, effectively 
exploiting synthetic lethality in HRD tumors. Under normal conditions, PARP 
enzymes are responsible for repairing single -strand DNA breaks, which are converted 
to doubl e-strand breaks during DNA replication. In HRP cells, these breaks are

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
tions, PARP 
enzymes are responsible for repairing single -strand DNA breaks, which are converted 
to doubl e-strand breaks during DNA replication. In HRP cells, these breaks are 
normally repaired by the HR pathway, while in HR -deficient tumors, this mechanism

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
13 
 
 is impaired, leading to DNA damage accumulation and cancer cell death (Caruso et al., 
2025) . 
Beyond PARP inhibitors, other targeted therapies include antiangiogenic drugs, such 
as bevacizumab. By inhibiting vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) signaling , 
bevacizumab limits tumor vascularization and provides significant clinical benefit 
when combined with chemotherapy (Zhang et al., 2025) . However, not all targeted 
therapies have clinical benefits in HGSC. While immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), 
such as those targeting TIGIT and PD -1/PD -L1 axis, have achieved remarkable success 
in solid tumors like melanoma and non -small cell lung cancer, they have shown only 
limited benefit in restoring immunit y in HGSC tumors. This is largely attributed to the 
immunologically “cold” tumor microenvironment of HGSC with low immune 
infiltration and high immune suppression. Current efforts focus on improvi ng targeted 
therapies’ potential through therapy combinations and better patient stratification 
(Pawłowska et al., 2022) . 
Despite these therapeutic advances, the primary obstacle in HGSC treatment remains 
the emergence of chemoresistance and subsequent disease recurrence. In addition to 
DNA damage repair defects, resistance and recurrence are driven by alterations in the

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
remains 
the emergence of chemoresistance and subsequent disease recurrence. In addition to 
DNA damage repair defects, resistance and recurrence are driven by alterations in the 
drug transporters' expression, oxidative stress resistance, and the presence of cancer 
stem cells (CSCs). 
Crucially, dysregulation of the cell cycle is central to cancer biology. It serves as an 
important prognostic factor and driver of treatment failure. Because the loss of normal 
cell cycle control allows tumor cells to sustain proliferative signaling and evade growth 
suppressors, understanding these mechanisms is essential for addressing 
chemoresistance. For instance, in HRP cases of HGSC, 20% of tumors have 
amplification or gain of the cyclin E1 ( CCNE1 ) gene, which promotes rapid progression 
through the cell cycle and is associated with platinum resistance. Conversely, loss of 
the retinoblastoma (RB) protein, a master regulator of the G1 -to-S phase transition, is 
associated with higher survival rates and platinum sensitivity. Ultimately, thes e 
molecular alterations suggest that the tumor’s ability to survive therapy is linked to 
how it manages cell cycle progression and checkpoints.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
14 
 
 2.3 Cell cycle 
The mitotic cell cycle is a tightly regulated process, ensuring accurate division of 
genetic material and formation of two identical daughter cells. It is divided into two 
stages: interphase and mitosis (Matthews et al., 2021) . Interphase consists of G1, S, 
and G2 phases (Fig. 2) . During these phases , the cell grows, replicates genetic material , 
and prepares for mitosis. DNA replication happens in the S phase, flanked by the G1 
and G2 gap phases . These gap phases provide the necessary time for cellular growth 
and the synthesis of proteins required for the subsequent division. 
 
Figure 2. Regulatory network of the cell cycle. The diagram illustrates the progression through G1, S, 
G2, and M phases. Key regulatory steps include the phosphorylation of RB by Cyclin D -CDK4/6 and 
Cyclin E -CDK2, leading to the release of E2F/DP for the transcripti on of S -phase genes. Inhibitory 
pathways involving the Cip/Kip (p21, p27, p57) and INK4 (p15, p16, p18, p19) families are shown with 
T-bars. Diagram created by Gemini. 
Progression through the cell cycle is guided by cyclin -dependent kinases (CDKs) in 
coordination with their regulatory partners, cyclins, which coordinat e CDKs and 
ensure the transitions between cell cycle phases. The decision to enter the cell cycle 
happens in the G1 phase.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ith their regulatory partners, cyclins, which coordinat e CDKs and 
ensure the transitions between cell cycle phases. The decision to enter the cell cycle 
happens in the G1 phase. Here, the cell decides to continue the cell cycle or exit to the 
quiescent G 0 phase. In the G1 phase, cyclin D binds CDK4/6 and activates it (Song et 
al., 2024) . Activated CDK4/6 binds and phosphorylates the RB protein, which triggers 
the release of the E2F transcription factor s. Released E2F transcription factors activate 
the expression of genes required for DNA replication and produc tion of cyclin E. Cyclin 
E-CDK2 complex phosphorylates RB further and drives the entry to S phase.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
15 
 
 Once the decision to enter the cell cycle has been made, cyclin A -CDK2 complexes drive 
the initiation and progression of DNA replication. The accumulation of cyclins E and 
A, coupled with the inactivation of the APC/CCDH1 complex, enables initiation of 
replication . Finally, the transition to mitosis is driven by CDK1. It activates key kinases 
and triggers the chromosomal and cellular changes needed for cell division. 
While cell cycle checkpoints operate as a DNA integrity tracking mechanism, ensuring 
propagation of DNA without errors through multiple generations, cancer cells 
frequently bypass these signals. The loss of certain cell cycle checkpoints causes cancer 
cells to become heavily dependent on other cell cycle control mechanisms . This 
prevent s catastrophic damage to genetic material and ensure s cell viability. 
Consequently, this dysregulation is not just a biological anomaly but a defining feature 
of the disease. Specifically, three of the defined Hallmarks of Cancer are directly linked 
to the loss of cell cycle control (Hanahan, 2026) : 
• Sustaining proliferative signaling : Cancer cells bypass normal growth 
factor requirements to maintain continuous division. 
• Evading growth suppressors : Tumors often disable the proteins, which 
normally stop the cycle at the G1 checkpoint.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
owth 
factor requirements to maintain continuous division. 
• Evading growth suppressors : Tumors often disable the proteins, which 
normally stop the cycle at the G1 checkpoint. 
• Enabling replicative immortality : Cells detour programmed senescence to 
divide indefinitely. 
 
2.4 Cell cycle dysregulation and therapeutic opportunities in cancer 
This continuous progression and failure to exit the cell cycle make malignant cells 
specifically vulnerable to therapies targeting the remaining cell cycle machinery 
(Matthews et al., 2021) . Beyond simple proliferation, cancer cells utilize additional 
mechanisms to navigate stressful conditions and evade the normal cell cycle 
checkpoints . These mechanisms include checkpoint recovery, where cells over ride 
DNA damage signals , ”resetting ” the cell cycle clock to resume division despite the 
damage (Min & Lee, 2023; L. Zhu et al., 2015) . Furthermore, tumor cells may enter 
quiescent or dormant states, shiftin g into a reversible G0 phase that metabolically 
protects them from chemotherapies targeting active DNA synthesis. The evasion of 
cell cycle regulation is further achieved by circumventing senescence and apoptosis 
through the inactivation of tumor suppressors such as TP53 and RB1. Finally , tumors

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
16 
 
 often develop mechanisms to bypass replication stress . Cells manage stalled 
replication forks by utilizing translesion synthesis or activating dormant replication 
origins. 
The biological significance of these adaptations is particularly evident when 
considering that the most efficient and widely used cancer treatment strategies rely on 
the induction of excessive DNA damage. Because these therapeutic strategies aim to 
overwhelm the cell's repair capacity, any mechanism that allows a tumor cell to delay, 
bypass, or tolerate such damage becomes a primary driver of clinical failure. 
In HGSC , platinum -based therapies remain the cornerstone of the treatment. The se 
agents form DNA crosslinks, disrupting the replication and triggering cell death. 
Similarly, in other malignancies such as breast, lung , and hematologic cancers , DNA -
damaging agents , including doxorubicin and topoisomerase inhibitors , are used. 
However, the cellular adaptations described directly counteract these interventions. 
Consequently, the treatment acts as a selective pressure, facilitating the emergence and 
survival of chemo resistant populations. 
Traditional chemotherapies can be broadly classified into three groups (Song et al., 
2024) : 
• Drugs affecting DNA biosynthesis : They primarily act during the S phase

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ations. 
Traditional chemotherapies can be broadly classified into three groups (Song et al., 
2024) : 
• Drugs affecting DNA biosynthesis : They primarily act during the S phase 
by interfering with nucleoside synthesis, either by mimicking nucleosides (e.g., 
fluorouracil), inhibiting nucleotide formation ( e.g., methotrexate), or blocking 
DNA polymerase activity ( e.g., cytarabine). 
• Drugs affecting DNA structure and function : They also mainly target the 
S phase by disrupting DNA integrity through alkylation, strand breakage, or 
formation of intra - and interstrand crosslinks (e.g., platinum -based agents, 
cyclophosphamide). 
• Drugs affecting RNA and protein biosynthesi s and mitosis: They act 
across the S and M phases . They function by binding DNA and interfering with 
transcription or mitotic spindle dynamics (e.g., taxanes, vincristine, 
adriamycin).

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
17 
 
 To overcome the limitations of these broad -spectrum agents, t argeted cell cycle 
therapies have emerged as a promising alternative . They offer the advantages of low 
toxicity, high er target specificity, and fewer adverse side effects (Song et al., 2024) . 
Furthermore, unlike traditional DNA -damaging chemotherap ies, they don’t introduce 
mutations to the DNA, decreas ing the chances of mutation -driven drug resistance. 
Current targeted strategies focus on disrupting key cell -cycle checkpoints: 
• G1/S checkpoint : Interventions at this checkpoint either interfere with DNA -
damage sensing Ataxia telangietasia mutation (ATM) related pathways ( e.g., 
ATM -Chk2 -p53 signaling ) or directly inhibit the cyclin -dependent kinases 
driving cell cycle progression, such as CDK2 and CDK4/6 
• G2/M checkpoint : This checkpoint monitors DNA damage before the mito tic 
entry and inactivates CDK 1 in case of damage . Therapies here target kinases 
such as CHK1, Wee1, and CDK1, collectively disrupting the ATR -Chk1 -Wee1 
axis. This forces damaged cells into premature mitosis and following mitotic 
catastrophe . 
• Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC) : Interventions target core SAC 
components such as BUB1, BUBR1, MAD2, CENP -E, and CDC20 to disrupt 
mitotic fidelity and induce cell death during division.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
bly Checkpoint (SAC) : Interventions target core SAC 
components such as BUB1, BUBR1, MAD2, CENP -E, and CDC20 to disrupt 
mitotic fidelity and induce cell death during division. 
While the therapeutic strategies detailed above specifically target the machinery of the 
cell cycle, their clinical efficacy is rarely uniform across the entire tumor. The ability of 
a cancer cell to utilize survival adaptations is not dictated solely by its internal genetic 
makeup. Rather, these cell cycle decisions are heavily influenced by the spatial 
architecture and biochemical signaling of the surrounding tissue microenvironment . 
To understand why specific populations can evade these potent therapies while others 
succumb, the focus must shift from the individual tumor cells to the complex ecosystem 
that sustain s them. 
2.5 Tumor microenvironment 
The understanding of cancer has greatly evolved over the last two decades. Initially 
being considered simple spheroid assemblies of malignant cells, tumors are now 
acknowledged as highly complex ecosystems, comprising tumor cells, immune cells, 
extracellular matrix (ECM), cancer -associated fibroblasts (CAFs), vascular system , and

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
18 
 
 various less abundant constituents . Collectively, t his whole cancer ecosystem is known 
as the tumor microenvironment (TME). 
 
Figure 3. Cellular heterogeneity and spatial architecture of the TME . Schematic representation of the 
diverse cellular populations and ecological interactions within the TME. Diagram created with BioRender 
(2026). 
The TME plays a n active role in every stage of cancer progression, from tumor 
initiation and progression to invasion , intravasation , and eventual metastatic 
dissemination (Anderson & Simon, 2020) . The critical role of TME becomes evident 
from the eff icacy of therapies that target its stromal and immune compartments , 
rather than relying on direct cytotoxicity against the cancer cells. Malignant cells 
actively orchestrate th is microenvironment by recruiting and reprogramming non -
cancerous cells, which subsequently adopt designated role s that can heavily influence 
patient prognosis . 
The immune landscape of the tumor is particularly important . Tumors are broadly 
classified into “immune -infiltrated ” (often termed “immune -hot”) , “immune-
excluded, and “immune -silent ” phenotypes (Anderson & Simon, 2020) . This 
immunological profile is often dictated by the underlying tumor genetic phenotype.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
19 
 
 For instance, in HGSC, tumors with BRCA1/2 mutations typically exhibit an 
”immun e-hot” profile, while BRCA -wildtype tumors are more often ”immune -silent ” 
or ”immune -excluded”. 
Within the TME, the dynamic interactions between tumor and immune cells directly 
determine the course of the disease. High infiltration of CD8+ cytotoxic T cells and 
CD4+ helper T cells into the TME is generally associated with better prognosis , as 
CD8+ cells directly kill tumor cells and inhibit angiogenesis , while CD4+ helper T cells 
support cytotoxic responses . In contrast, the presence of regulatory T cells promote s 
tumor progression by suppressing anti -tumor immune responses. Similarly, tumor -
associated macrophages, which often represent the most abundant immune 
population within the TME , can adopt M1 (pro -inflammatory) and M2 (anti -
inflammatory) state s and exhibit a context -dependent role through this polarization . 
Usually, macrophages are polarized towards the M2 state , which promotes 
immunosuppression and is associat ed with poor clinical outcomes . 
Beyond the immune compartment, the TME is supported by non -immune stroma. It 
includes endothelial cells, CAFs , adipocytes , and the structural network of ECM . These 
components interact with both tumor and immune cells, regulating signaling 
molecules and nutrient delivery , and facilitating migration.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
and the structural network of ECM . These 
components interact with both tumor and immune cells, regulating signaling 
molecules and nutrient delivery , and facilitating migration. For instance, CAFs secrete 
TGF -β, inducing epithelial -to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) of the tumor cells and 
modify ing the ECM density . This dense matrix can physically barrier immune cells 
from infiltrating the tumor, thereby directly contributing to therapy resistance and 
poor clinical outcomes ( Yang et al., 2023). 
HGSC is considered a particularly heterogeneous type of cancer. The composition and 
state of the TME vary between the patients and greatly influence the prognosis. 
Moreover, i n HGSC , tumor phenotypes are not solely determined by intrinsic genomic 
alterations. Recent studies demonstrate that specific molecular features of HGSC are 
consistently associated with specific immune and stromal landscapes. For example, 
HGSC tumors with MHCII expression exhibit enhanced antigen presentation capacity 
and are associate d with spatially organized and immune -infiltrated “hot” 
microenvironments, which directly correlate with improved clinical outcomes (Perez -
Villatoro et al., n.d.) .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
20 
 
 Beyon d immune regulation, the TME actively dictates tumor cell behavior thr ough 
complex programs. K ey biological survival programs such as EMT , cancer stemness, 
and tumor dormancy are sustained by specific stromal signals. While EMT is driven by 
increased fibroblast activation and extracellular matrix remodeling , promoting 
immunosuppressive niches, stem -like and dormant tumor cell populations are 
supported by specific , localized stromal signals enabling their persistence . Altogether, 
these findings highlight that tumor cell states and TME co -evolve in HGSC, jointly 
shaping disease progression and treatment response. 
Despite the critical importance of this co -evolution, the intricate signaling and 
architecture of the TME cannot be captured by traditional bulk and single -cell 
analys es. These conventional methods require tissue dissociation, losing the critical 
context of cellular neighbo rhoods . Investigating how these localized niches drive 
therapy resistance necessitates a shift toward spatial technologies . These methods 
preserve the native relationship between the malignant and stromal components. 
2.6 Spatial resolution methodologies 
Spatial resolution methodologies have greatly evolved over the past two decades. They 
enable precise mapping of biomolecules within the tissues, linking tissue architecture 
with molecular information.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
hodologies have greatly evolved over the past two decades. They 
enable precise mapping of biomolecules within the tissues, linking tissue architecture 
with molecular information. Specifically, spatial proteomics provide s quantitative, 
localiz ed data on protein abundance , while spatial transcriptomics reveal s gene 
expression patterns across tissues. Integrating these m odalities provides a powerful 
framework for understanding biological development, disease progression, and 
therapeutic response. 
2.7 Spatial transcriptomics 
Spatial transcriptomics approaches enable the identification of cell types and states, 
the determin ation of spatially variable gene expression, and the detailed 
characterization of cell neighborhoods (Jain & Eadon, 2024) . Early attempts to create 
spatial transcriptomics methods date back to the 1960s , relying on hybridization of 
radioactively labelled DNA or RNA to tissue sections , followed by recording the signal 
with autoradiography. Currently, there is a wide range of spatial transcriptomics 
methodologies available , providing spatial data with varying degrees of resolution, 
sequencing depth, and genomic coverage (see Table 1) .

Spatial transcriptomics technologies can be broadly divided into two groups: 
sequencing -based and imaging -based approaches. 
2.7.1 Sequencing -based spatial transcriptomics 
Sequencing -based spatial transcriptomics technologies utilize next -generation 
sequencing and DNA barcoding to map spatial transcriptomics data across the tissue 
sample . They are further subdivided to in situ capture (ISC) methods and region -of-
interest (ROI) -based sequencing. 
• ISC methods 
ISC methods operate by hybridizing the tissue to a slide with the barcoded 
probes attached. Upon tissue permeabilization, RNA is released from the tissue

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
22 
 
 and binds to these capture probes. The most prominent technologies 
representing ISC methods include Visium, Slide -seq and Stereo -seq. They 
provide varying resolution, ranging from subcellular to roughly 25 cells per 
sequencing spot . However, these methods generally require fresh -frozen tissue 
for optimal performance . 
• ROI -based sequencing 
ROI -based sequencing is an alternative to ISC -based methods. This approach 
involves the supervised , morphologically guided selection of ROIs from stained 
tissues . The ROI selection is followed by the collection of the cells from the 
selected ROI s into a microplate for downstream sequencing . ROI -based 
sequencing methods have the advantages of lower cost and the ability to target 
specific tissue areas based on functional or pathological features. 
GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (DSP) is the premier example of this approach. 
It enables ROI -guided , highly customizable molecular profiling of tissues. The 
coverage of genes is comprehensive and comparable to ISC technologies 
(around 18000 genes). It is important to note, h owever, that ROI -based 
methods lack single -cell resolution and introduce user bias du ring the manual 
ROI selection step. 
2.7.2 Imaging -based spatial transcriptomics 
Imaging -based spatial transcriptomics methods rely on direct visualization of RNA

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ser bias du ring the manual 
ROI selection step. 
2.7.2 Imaging -based spatial transcriptomics 
Imaging -based spatial transcriptomics methods rely on direct visualization of RNA 
molecules within the tissue sections using fluorescence . Imaging -based spatial 
transcriptomics approaches are primarily divided into situ sequencing (ISS) and in situ 
hybridization (ISH) approaches. 
• In Situ Sequencing (ISS) 
ISS relies on reverse transcription of mRNA into cDNA in situ , followed by 
sequencing through rolling circle amplification . The sequences are decoded by 
recording the fluorescence from the latterly added, labeled nucleotides. These 
methods usually involve the preselection of target genes. 
• In Situ Hybridization (ISH) -based methods

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
23 
 
 ISH-based technologies rely on the direct hybridization of fluorescently labe led 
probes to mRNA molecules with in the tissue and microscopically recording the 
fluorescence. While t hese methods enable subcellular recording of the signal , 
they are limited by the need for predetermined target panels . Representative 
platforms include CosMx, Xenium, MERFISH, and seqFISH+. 
2.7.3 GeoMx digital spatial profiling 
Representing a prominent example of the ROI -based sequencing technologies 
introduced in Section 2.7.1, GeoMx DSP is a method for highly multiplex profiling of 
proteins and RNA, highly compatible with FFPE samples (Merritt et al., 2020) . It 
enables profiling of both RNA and protein s within the preselected ROI . To achieve this, 
it utilizes a variety of mRNA probes or antibodies conjugated with UV -photocleavable 
oligonucleotide barcodes (Fig. 4). 
The general workflow proceeds as follows: 
1. Following the antigen retrieval, fluorescent morphology markers are applied to 
the tissue sections to visualize the underlying tissue architecture . 
2. Next, the slides are loaded into the DSP and scanned for immunofluorescence 
markers to guide the selection of ROIs for the experiment. Following the 
imaging and ROI selection, the ROIs are segmented into areas of illumination 
(AOIs) based on morphological features , defining the exact cellular

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
the experiment. Following the 
imaging and ROI selection, the ROIs are segmented into areas of illumination 
(AOIs) based on morphological features , defining the exact cellular 
compartments from which the sequencing will occur. 
3. Then, the oligonucleotides from the probes get photocleaved with focused UV 
light . These released oligonucleotides are collected and quantified using the 
nCounter system.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
24 
 
 
Figure 4. Overview of the GeoMx DSP workflow and RNA validation approaches. (a) Schematic of 
GeoMx DSP probe architectur e, illustrating target -specific oligonucleotides conjugated to indexing 
oligo s via UV -photocleavable . (b) Methodological c omparison of GeoMx DSP alongside standard 
validation techniques: RNAscope and the nCounter . Adapted from Merritt et al. (2020) . 
2.8 Spatial proteomics 
Spatial proteomics is revolutionizing our understanding of biological processes by 
exposing how tissue architectures are formed at the protein level. This field provide s 
critical insight s into the tissue microenvironment, driving discoveries in cancer biology 
and beyond . Spatial proteomics methods can be broadly categorized into three groups: 
liquid chromatography -tandem mass spectrometry (LC -MS/MS) -based, imaging mass 
spectrometry (MS) -based, and antibody -based methods (see Table 2) (Hu et al., 2025) .

2.8.1 Antibody -based spatial proteomics 
Antibody -based methods rely on signal molecules linked to antibodies to determine 
precise protein localization within tissues . The se methods are limited by the relatively 
low number of targets that can be detected in a single experiment. To address this, 
multiplexed antibody -based methods have been developed, which can be categorized 
into fluorophore -labeled, DNA oligonucleotide -labeled , and metal isotope -labeled 
methods. 
• Fluorophore -labeled methods 
Typically involve s multiple rounds of staining, image acquisition, and 
fluorophore inactivation. While t hese techniques offer high spatial resolution 
and excellent subcellular localization of proteins , the scalability of such methods 
is limited . Challenges include altered antigenicity, incomplete fluorescence 
quenching, cross -reactivity of antibodies, and the difficulty of resolving protein 
abundance s that vary across several order s of magnitude. 
• DNA -oligonucleotide -labeled methods 
Significantly improve scalability and throughput . These approaches typically 
involve a single staining ste p with oligonucleotide -conjugated antibodies, 
followed by cyclic hybridization and removal of fluorescently labeled probes for 
readout.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ypically 
involve a single staining ste p with oligonucleotide -conjugated antibodies, 
followed by cyclic hybridization and removal of fluorescently labeled probes for 
readout. 
• Metal -labe led antibody methods 
Conjugate antibodies with metal isotopes , coupling the process with mass 
spectrometry readout . Following a single round of staining, the metal tags are 
released from the tissue sample and detected by a mass spectrometer. This 
allows the detection of over 40 markers with high resolution and speed. 
However, these techniques are destructive to the sample , prevent ing 
downstream analysis of the same tissue section.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
26 
 
 2.8.2 LC–MS/MS -based spatial proteomics 
LC-MS/MS -based spatial proteomics combines liqui d chromatography -tandem mass 
spectrometry with precision microdissection techniques , such as laser capture 
microdissection. Tissues are either divided into small , standardized regions (“voxels” ), 
or ROIs are specifically exci sed for profiling, requiring fewer protein samples and 
resources. 
2.8.3 Imaging MS-based approaches 
Finally, imaging MS-based approaches enable untargeted profiling of protein 
distribution within the tissue. In these techniques, t he mass spectrum of charged 
molecules is recorded systematically across the tissue surface , allowing researchers to 
generate spatial maps of molecular protein abundance. 
2.8.4 Tissue -based cyclic immunofluorescence 
As a prominent example of antibody -based spatial proteomics, t-CyCIF creates a 
multiplexed image by collecting low -plex images from the same sample in cycles and 
then assembling them into one high -dimensional representation (Lin Jia -Ren et al., 
2018) . Th is fluorophore -labeled technique allows for multiplexing up to 60 protein 
markers. 
The standard t -CyCIF workflow begins with sections cut from FFPE blocks, followed 
by dewaxing and antigen retrieval. The pre -staining with secondary antibodies is 
performed to reduce the autofluorescence (Fig. 5). The pre -staining is followed by a

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
followed 
by dewaxing and antigen retrieval. The pre -staining with secondary antibodies is 
performed to reduce the autofluorescence (Fig. 5). The pre -staining is followed by a 
chemical bleaching , including oxidation in a high -pH hydrogen peroxide and light 
exposure. All subsequent cycles follow the iterative process : 1) staining with antibodies 
against protein antigens , 2) counter staining with DNA dye , 3) imaging , and 4) 
fluorophore bleaching. Notabl y, the signal -to-noise ratio is increased with successive 
cycle s due to the reduction of intrinsic tissue autofluorescence. Finally, the 
computational image processing steps include illumination correction and stitching to 
generate the final multiplexed image .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
27 
 
 
Figure 5. Schematic of the t -CyCIF workflow. A conceptual overview illustrating pre-processing of the 
tissue , iterative cycles of staining and bleaching, and downstream computational image analysis. 
Adapted from Lin Jia -Ren et al., (2018).

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
28 
 
 Aims and objectives of the study 
The goal of this study is to characterize the molecular features and mechanisms 
inherent to highly proliferative “susbister ” niches in post -NACT samples of HGSC. In 
particular, the study focused on identifying biological programs enabling tumor cell 
chemotherapy subsistence. To achieve this, the study had the following objectives: 
• To integrate t -CyCIF and GeoMx to map highly proliferative “subsister ” tumor 
niches and annotate these hubs in paired GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data. 
• To identify the gene expression programs and signaling pathways operating in 
chemotherapy -subsisting tumor niches 
• To identify key biological processes and pathways that may contribute to 
therapy resistance . 
These analyses provided insights into the mechanisms supporting chemotherapy 
subsistence and highlighted potential therapeutic vulnerabilities, with relevance for 
improving treatment strategies in patients with resistant HGSC.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
29 
 
 3 Material and Methods 
3.1 Cohort description for t-CyCIF experiment 
Cohort data were obtained from participants in the prospective ONCOSYS -OVA 
clinical trial. Th is clinical trial was conducted at the University of Helsinki and Helsinki 
University Hospital. This trial facilitated the collection of surgical specimens and 
detailed clinicopathological data, including variables such as age, tumor histology, and 
FIGO tumor stage. Additional clinical variables, such as progression -free survival 
(PFS), PFI, and overall survival (OS), are available for all patients. The study was 
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic at Helsinki University 
Hospital and Helsinki Biobank. Patient identifiers and personal information in the 
clinical data are securely stored on the HUH server with access restricted to authorized 
personnel only. 
Interval debulking surgery (IDS) specimens were collected from patients who had 
undergone neoadjuvant chemotherapy treatment (NACT) (N=69) and were diagnosed 
with the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) stage III -IV 
HGSC . 
The cohort includes a subset of samples previously profiled with GeoMx and Xenium 
spatial transcriptomics technologies (Fig. 6). Samples that have not been previously 
profiled were included if derived from the metastatic omentum , exhibited a tumor

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nd Xenium 
spatial transcriptomics technologies (Fig. 6). Samples that have not been previously 
profiled were included if derived from the metastatic omentum , exhibited a tumor 
purity greater than 10% , and were free of hemorrhage or necrosis. The se strict 
inclusion criteria were designed to ensure a uniform tissue site and sufficient cancer 
cell material. A total of 69 samples were include d.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
30 
 
 
Figure 6. Workflow for t -CyCIF sample selection. Schematic representing the filtering process for 
OncosysOVA IDS samples. Selection was based on prior experimental use (GeoMx/Xenium), 
anatomical site (omentum), and histopathological assessment (tumor purity > 10% an d absence of 
necrosis or hemorrhage). Flowchart created with BioRender (2026). 
3.2 Sample preparation and marker selection for t-CyCIF experiment 
For spatial and molecular profiling, formalin -fixed paraffin -embedded (FFPE) tissue 
blocks were sectioned at a thickness of 5 m and placed on Superfrost slides . To ensure 
spatial concordance across different modalities, consecutive sections separated by 5 -
15 m were utilized for GeoMx and t -CyCIF analyses.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
31 
 
 The t-CyCIF slides were stained with a validated antibody panel across 7 cycles 
targeting 26 protein markers. Most markers were selected to identify cell types 
(stromal, immune, cancer) and assess functional cell cycle states. The markers 
included are displayed in Figure 7. 
 
Figure 7. Antibody panel for 7 -cycle t -CyCIF experiment . Summary of antibodies, target proteins, 
fluorophore channels, and validation details used for multiplexed imaging. 
3.3 t-CyCIF experiment 
3.3.1 Slide baking, deparaffinization, and rehydration 
Slides were incubated overnight in the oven at 37°C. On the following day, the slides 
were baked at 56°C for 1 hour. Next, they were deparaffinized and dehydrated through 
the xylene and alcohol series (xylene 3 × 5 min, followed by sequential rehydration 
immersions in 100%, 90%, 70%, 50%, and 30% ethanol 3 min each). Finally, the slides 
were washed twice in 1×PBS (2 × 1 min) to complete the rehydration and prepare the 
tissue for further staining.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
32 
 
 3.3.2 Antigen retrieval 
Antigen retrieval was done with a freshly prepared 10 mM citric acid buffer (pH 6.0 ). 
The process was conducted in a pressure cooker containing 550 mL of Milli -Q water in 
the main chamber and 225 mL of citric acid buffer in each slide rack holder. The 
antigen retrieval was performed using a preset protocol at 95°C for 45 min. Following 
retrieval, t he slides and the citric acid buffer were transferred to a new rack holder and 
cooled under the fume hood . Once the slides had cooled, they were washed in 1×PBS 
(3 × 3 min ). 
3.3.3 Pre-staining 
Pre-staining was done in a humidified chamber . Sections were incubated with 200 µL 
of Odyssey blocking buffer for 30 min at room temperature. Following blocking , the 
slides were incubated for 60 minutes at room temperature with 200 µ L of a pre -
staining cocktail containing secondary antibodies and Hoechst (see Table 3) .

A bleaching solution was prepared consisting of 1 20 mL of 1×PBS , 22.5 mL of 30% 
H₂O₂, 7.5 mL of 480 mM NaOH . Slides were immersed in this solution within a 
transparent rack and placed on an LED light source. Photobleaching was performed 
for 90 min at room temperature, changing the buffer solution every 30 min. Following 
photobleaching, the s lides were washed in 1×PBS (4 × 3 min ). 
3.3.4 Primary and secondary antibody staining 
The slides were incubated with primary unconjugated antibodies overnight at 4°C. 
Following the incubation, t he slides were washed with 1×PBS (3 × 3 min ). Secondary 
antibodies (1:2000 dilution ) and Hoechst (1:1000 dilution ) in Odyssey buffer were

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
33 
 
 applied for 1 hour at RT. Next, they were washed in 1×PBS (3 × 3 min ). The area 
surrounding the tissue section was dried with fiber -free tissue paper. Slides were then 
mounted with 100 µL of 70% glycerol in 1 × PBS, cover slipped and scanned using a 
RareCyte CyteFinder scanner. 
3.3.5 Subsequent staining cycles 
Following the initial scan, coverslips were removed with 1×PBS. Next, slides 
underwent photobleach ing, changing the solution every 25 min. The slides were 
washed in 1×PBS (4 × 3 min ). Next, they were stained with pre -conjugated primary 
antibodies (recommended dilution) and Hoechst 1:10000 at 4°C. They were washed 3 
× 3 min with 1×PBS. Afterwards, the slides were mounted with 100 µL of 70% glycerol 
in 1 × PBS, coverslipped , and scanned . 
The iterative staining workflow was executed over seven full cycles, each comprising a 
sequence of chemical photobleaching, antibody incubation, and digital imaging. 
3.4 t-CyCIF image processing 
Image preprocessing and analysis were conducted through the sequence of steps , 
which includ ed illumination correction, image stitching, segmentation, signal 
quantification , phenotyping with TRIBUS, and downstream analyses (Kang et al., 
2025) . Illumination correction was performed with BaSic, an image correction method 
based on low -rank and sparse decomposition (Peng et al., 2017) . Image stitching and

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
al., 
2025) . Illumination correction was performed with BaSic, an image correction method 
based on low -rank and sparse decomposition (Peng et al., 2017) . Image stitching and 
registration were carried out using ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous 
Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency Registration) (Muhlich et al., n.d.) . ASHLAR is a 
multi -aimed open -source Python tool for image preprocessing. It performs stitching 
and align ment of adjacent tiles within each cycle of immunofluorescence. It also aids 
in aligning tiles from different cycles, covering the same region. Following that, cell 
segmentation was performed using StarDist (Schmidt et al., 2018) . Finally, the 
channel -specific signal intensities were quantified at the single -cell level to enable the 
phenotyping .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
34 
 
 
Figure 8. Multiplexed image processing and snalysis workflow. Overview of the computational pipeline, 
including illumination correction (BaSiC), image stitching (ASHLAR), cell segmentation (StarDist), signal 
quantification, and single -cell phenotyping (TRIBUS), followed by downstream image alignment (Warpy) 
and ROI annotation. Diagram created with BioRender (2026). 
3.5 t-CyCIF cell phenotyping 
Quantified t-CyCIF data were phenotyped using TRIBUS (Kang et al., 2025) . TRIBUS 
is a knowledge -based classifier for multiplexed images that allows single -cell 
phenotyping based on marker combinations in fluorescence images without relying on 
manual gating. 
For this study, cells were classified into “Tumor” and “Stroma” at the global level 
according to the predefined logic tables ( Fig. 9 ). In the second classification level, the 
tumor cells were further subdivided into four categories: “Tumor general”, “Tumor 
Ki67 +pRb +", “Tumor pRb +”, and “Tumor Ki67 +”. 
Next, to refine the tumor classifications, the “Tumor pRb +” and “Tumor -general” 
categories were merged. Additionally, the “Tumor pH3+” category was added, with a 
gating threshold of 600. Finally, the q uality control of phenotyping was performed 
using the integrated Napari widget (Chiu Chi -Li et al., 2022) .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
35 
 
 
 
Figure 9. TRIBUS logic -based phenotyping strategy. The table details the marker combinations used for 
global cell classification (Level 1) into Stroma and Tumor compartments, followed by the sub -
classification (Level 2) of tumor cells based on Ki67 and pRb expression. Values indicate the applied 
classification logic (1: positive, 0: neutral, -1: exclusionary). 
3.6 GeoMx spatial transcriptomics: ROI selection and data acquisition 
GeoMx spatial transcriptomics procedure was performed at the University of Helsinki 
(Färkkilä lab ) following the standard NanoString Technologies guidelines. The region 
of interest (ROI) selection was driven by specific cellular phenotypes identified via t -
CyCIF. 
For this study, we utilize d the subset of this data, comprising 305 ROIs obtained from 
33 samples . This subset includ ed 18 AOIs derived from tumor regions and 287 AOIs 
coming from the tumor -stroma interface ( TSI). 
3.7 GeoMx and t-CyCIF data pairing 
t-CyCIF and GeoMx images were aligned using the Warpy workflow (Chiaruttini et al., 
2022) . It is a Python -based framework designed for whole slide image (WSI) 
registration in Fiji and succeeding analy sis in QuPath. This workflow is designed to 
register slides using complex spline -based transformations with ImgLib library, 
elastix , and BigWarp.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
36 
 
 For the alignment process , the GeoMx was selected as a fixed source and t-CyCIF image 
as a moving source. The moving and fixed images were initially manually aligned 
within Fiji. Then, the rectangu lar registration region was selected. After choosing the 
number of landmarks within the rectangle , the Elastix component in Fiji executed the 
registration. The result of the registration was saved to the JSON file. 
Next , the ImageCombinerWarpy extension in QuPath was used to combine registered 
WSIs into a new QuPath image entry , using the registration information recorded in 
JSON file . The combined image allowed the transfer of the regions of interest from 
GeoMx to the t -CyCIF image. 
The custom Groovy -based scripts were used for the import of the original GeoMx ROI 
coordinates and mapping them on to the t-CyCIF image using the Warpy extension. 
Following this automated transfer, the manual quality control check of the ROI 
transfer was performed to ensure alignment accuracy . Finally, t he mapped ROI 
coordinates were transferred to the CSV file and the spatial accuracy was verified using 
Napari.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
37 
 
 
Figure 10. Image registration and ROI Transfer Workflow. Schematic representation of the Warpy -based 
pipeline for aligning GeoMx and t -CyCIF WSIs and transferring ROIs. Adapted from the official Warpy 
documentation (available at https://imagej.net/plugins/bdv/warpy/warpy ). 
3.8 GeoMx label transfer 
Marker -quantified data, along with the observations , were compiled into the AnnData 
object (Virshup et al., 2024) . Where applicable, e ach cell in the matrix was a nnotated 
with the ROI from the corresponding aligned GeoMx image. 
ROIs were selected across three distinct spatial regions: the TSI, the tumor core , and 
distant stroma. Based on Pan-Cytokeratin (PanCK) staining, e ach ROI was segmented 
into either one or two Areas of I llumination (AOIs) corresponding to tumor (PanCK+) 
and/or stroma (PanCK -) compartments.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
38 
 
 Tumor cell phenotypes within each ROI were evaluated , and AOIs were ranked 
according to the proportion and counts of highly proliferative cells. Proliferative niches 
were then stratified into quartiles, allowing for a direct comparison between the highly 
proliferative (highest quartile) and low proliferative (lowest quartile) niches in 
posterior analyses. 
3.9 Random Forest (RF) for identification of unique transcriptional programs 
Selection of pathways for hierarchi cal clustering was performed using a n RF 
architecture , implemented via the scikit -learn library in Python. To ensure robust 
model performance and address data imbalances, the following configurations were 
applied: 
• Ensemble Configuration: The RF model was constructed using the 
RandomForestClassifier from sklearn.ensemble library , using an ensemble of 
2,000 individual decision trees. 
• Feature Importance Ranking: Candidate features were evaluated and 
selected based on the Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI) . This metric, 
accessed via the feature_importances attribute, quantifies a feature's 
contribution to the model by calculating the total reduction in the optimization 
criterion (Gini impurity) across all nodes and trees in the forest. 
• Class Imbalance Mitigation: To account for uneven class distributions 
within the dataset, we employed the class_weight='balanced' parameter. It

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nodes and trees in the forest. 
• Class Imbalance Mitigation: To account for uneven class distributions 
within the dataset, we employed the class_weight='balanced' parameter. It 
automatically adjusts weights in the input data, ensuring the RF classifier 
remains sensitive to the minority class. 
3.10 Differential gene expression analysis and single -sample gene set 
enrichment analysis on GeoMx data 
To characterize the transcriptional architecture inherent to highly proliferative tumor 
niches, gene expression profiles were analyzed using raw GeoMx digital count 
matrices. This pseudo -bulk transcriptomic approach allowed for the assessment of the 
total niche environment, including both tumor cells and surrounding 
microenvironmental components. Following the rigorous quality control , raw counts 
were normalized using the DESeq2 framework and subjected to a variance stabilizing

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
39 
 
 transformation (VST) to correct the mean -variance relationship . Finally, the Harmony 
algorithm was used to reduce technical batch effects . 
Differential gene expression (DGE) analysis was performed on the VST -normalized 
pseudo -bulk data using a linear -mixed effects model ( LMM ). The use of LMM was 
essential to account for the hierarchi cal structure of the data, particularly the nesting 
of multiple AOIs within individual patient samples. For e ach gene, a model was fit -
using the lme4 package in R, implemented with the following formula: 
y ~ AOI_category + (1 | Sample) 
In this model, y represents the normalized expression values, while AOI_category serves 
as the fixed effect. To control patient -specific confounding, the Sample effect was 
treated as a random intercept . Model -derived log2 fold changes and p -values were 
extracted for each feature, and significant ly differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were 
prioritized for functional interpretation. 
To identify the functional programs active within the bulk niche environment, single -
sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA ) was utilized to calculate per-AOI 
enrichment scores . This analysis utilized the Hallmark, KEGG, BioCarta, and Gene 
Ontology Biological Process pathway collections from the Molecular Signatures 
Database.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
per-AOI 
enrichment scores . This analysis utilized the Hallmark, KEGG, BioCarta, and Gene 
Ontology Biological Process pathway collections from the Molecular Signatures 
Database. To determine which pathways were significantly enriched between the 
highly and low proliferative niches, an LMM was applied to th ese precomputed ssGSEA 
scores by utilizing the fix ed and random effect s parameters described previously. 
3.11 Gene set enrichment analysis on GeoMx data 
Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was performed using GSEA software v4.4.0 
(Broad Institute). Raw gene expression profiles from tumor AOIs were s tratified into 
two groups based on t-CyCIF spatial phenotyping annotation s - highly proliferative 
and low proliferative . 
Enrichment analysis was implemented using the C2 REACTOME gene set collection 
from MSigDB. Default GSEA parameters were applied, utilizing 1000 permutations , 
and permuting by phenotype. For each pathway, the normalized enrichment score 
(NES) and false discovery rate (FDR) were calculated. In addition, the percentage of 
genes contributing to the enrichment signal (leading edge subset) was computed to

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
40 
 
 evaluate pathway representation. Pathways with an FDR < 0. 05 were considered 
significantly enriched. 
Code Availability 
The pipeline was written in Python and R. The code is available in GitHub . 
Link: https://github.com/randmikhailava/Master_thesis_Multiomics_Profiling 
Ethics Approval 
This study adhered to the Declaration of Helsinki and the ethical guidelines established 
by the University of Helsinki and Helsinki University Hospital (HUS). Pursuant to the 
Finnish Act on the Secondary Use of Health and Social Data (552/2019), informed 
consent was waived due to the use of pseudonymized archival materials. All data were 
stored securely within the University’s secure computing environment in str ict 
compliance with the GDPR and the Finnish Data Protection Act (1050/2018). The 
research follow ed the Responsible Conduct of Research (RCR) guidelines by the 
Finnish Advisory Board on Research Integrity (TENK).
