 4 Results 
4.1 High -plex spatial proteomi cs captures cell cycle and phenotypic cell states 
in post-NACT HGSC 
To investigate the spatial organization of tumor cell populations following NACT, a t-
CyCIF experiment was performed on a cohort of 69 post -NACT HGSC samples derived 
from 6 4 patients (Fig. 11) . High -quality multiplexed imaging data w ere successfully 
generated across 6 6 viable samples (three tissues were excluded during the experiment 
due to poor tissue quality). This comprehensive dataset captured a wide range of tissue 
architectures, enabling the assessment of both intra - and inter -patient heterogeneity 
in tumor composition and phenotypic states. 
 
Figure 11. t-CyCIF Cohort Overview. This heatmap confirms successful spatial proteomics profiling for 
the final analytical cohort of 6 9 samples. All specimens were collected at IDS following NACT to 
specifically study the "subsister" cell landscape. While samples primarily originate from the metastatic 
omentum, the cohort also includes adnexal and ovarian sites. 
Building upon our initial exploration of highly proliferative Ki67 +pRb + niches, we 
expanded the t -CyCIF antibody panel to comprehensively assess critical biological 
programs within the TME (Fig. 12) . The spatial expression of these markers assisted in 
precise tumor phenotypi ng. Most importantly , the expanded panel successfully

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
iological 
programs within the TME (Fig. 12) . The spatial expression of these markers assisted in 
precise tumor phenotypi ng. Most importantly , the expanded panel successfully 
resolve d cell cycle states across the tissue landscape , identify ing cell cycle quiescence 
(p27) and the G1/S transition (Cyclin D1 (CCND1) ), complementing standard Ki67 and 
pRb profil ing. Furthermore, the newly introduced markers successfully delineated 
EMT (captured via E-cadherin and N-cadherin) , stress response (c-Jun) , stem ness 
(ZIC2) , and DNA damage (gH2AX) .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
42 
 
 
Figure 12. Validation and Spatial Analysis of Expanded t -CyCIF Markers. Representative 
immunofluorescence images from optimized t -CyCIF experiments demonstrating specific staining 
patterns for expanded markers across marked biological states. The panel validates markers for key 
tumor programs: p27 (cell cycle quiescence, G 0 state), ZIC2 (stem -like features), gH2AX (DNA damage 
response), and N-Cadherin and E-Cadherin (epithelial -mesenchymal transition, EMT). The CCND 1 
images show successful optimization, resolving di stinct cytoplasmic and nuclear localization patterns 
associated with the G 1/S transition without confounding antibody aggregation. The c-Jun stain marks 
cells under a localized stress response. All images are composites with marker signal (colored) overlaid 
on DAPI nuclear stain (grey). 
4.2 Spatial cell phenotyping reveals substantial interpatient heterogeneity in 
the distribution of proliferative tumor cell states 
TRIBUS classification of quantified t-CyCIF data revealed a clear segregation of tumor 
and stromal compartments based on predefined marker logic (Fig. 9) . Within the 
tumor compartment, we observed additional phenotypic heterogeneit y, resolving cells 
into proliferative phenotypic states : Tumor Ki67 +, Tumor Ki67 +pRb +, and Tumor 
pH3 +. 
Firstly , cells were classified into tumor and stromal compartments. Posterior sub-

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
lls 
into proliferative phenotypic states : Tumor Ki67 +, Tumor Ki67 +pRb +, and Tumor 
pH3 +. 
Firstly , cells were classified into tumor and stromal compartments. Posterior sub-
classification of tumor cells identified multiple phenotypes, including Tumor -general, 
Tumor Ki67 +, Tumor Ki67 +pRb +, and a rare Tumor pH3+ positive proliferative 
subset . 
Quality control using integrated Napari visualization confirmed the robustness of 
phenotype assign ments and their alignment with protein marker expression . Together, 
these results demonstrate that our phenotyping approach resolves proliferative 
heterogeneity within tumor compartments and captures proliferative tumor cell states, 
providing the basis for downstream spatial transcriptomics analyses.

vector_chunks.text:
43 
 
 To assess inter -patient heterogeneity, the proportions of TRIBUS -defined tumor 
phenotypes were quantified across individual patients. A stacked barplot of cellular 
composition revealed marked variability in tumor subpopulation distributions 
between patients (Fig. 13) . Individual patients exhibited different compositional 
profiles o f tumor phenotypic states. While proliferative tumor phenotypes accounted 
for up to 58% of all cells in some samples , other samples contained less than 1% of 
proliferative tumor cells , demonstrating substantial inter -patient heterogeneity. 
Overall, substantial inter -patient variability was observed in the proportions of 
TRIBUS -defined tumor cell phenotypes. 
 
Figure 13. Inter-patient Heterogeneity in Tumor and Proliferative Cell Composition. Stacked barplot 
illustrating the relative proportions of TRIBUS -defined and manually gated (pH3+) cell phenotypes 
across the patient cohort. Samples are ordered by total tumor proportion , highlighting inter-patient 
variability in the distribution of proliferative tumor cell states. 
4.3 Alignment of spatial transcriptomics and proteomics enables the multi -
omic spatial mapping of "subsister" niches displaying marked proliferation 
profiles 
To ensure downstream analysis focused on the tumor, ROIs transferred from the

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
es the multi -
omic spatial mapping of "subsister" niches displaying marked proliferation 
profiles 
To ensure downstream analysis focused on the tumor, ROIs transferred from the 
GeoMx dataset to t-CyCIF images were restricted to tumor and TSI ROIs. The initial 
distribution of tumor cell counts across ROIs varied significantly (0-350 cells per ROI). 
To reduce the impact of low-tumor density regions, we excluded the bottom 10th 
percentile of (n= 34), with a minimum threshold of 12 tumor cells per ROI. 
The remaining ROIs were classified into four groups based on the relative proportion s 
and absolute counts of tumor cells representing Ki67 +pRb + highly proliferative tumor

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
44 
 
 niches. Specifically, ROIs were stratified into four groups: (i) high proportion /high 
coun ts (highly proliferative) (n=122) , (ii) high proportion /low counts (n=31) , (iii) low 
proportion /high counts (n=35) , and (iv) low proportion /low counts (low proliferative) 
(n=117) . This metric enabled the identification of ROIs enriched in proliferative states 
while accounting for variability in ROI size. Notably, the majority of AOIs originated 
from TSI regions. 
For downstream analyses, we focused on the phenotypic extremes (highly vs. low 
proliferative) to maximize biological contrast. Voronoi -based spatial analysis further 
supported this classification, revealing unique architectural features between these 
niches (Fig. 14). Specifically, h ighly proliferative niches show dense clusters of actively 
proliferati ng tumor cells, wh ereas low proliferative niches consist ed of non-
proliferative tumor cell populations . 
 
Figure 14. Voronoi diagrams illustrating spatial cell -type distribution in low versus highly proliferative 
tumor niches. Each polygon represents an individual cell, colored by its phenotype. The left panel 
demonstrates a low proliferative tumor niche predominantly populated by the general population of 
tumor cells (blue) and stroma (grey). The right panel highlights a highly proliferative neighborhood

vector_chunks.text:
oliferative tumor niche predominantly populated by the general population of 
tumor cells (blue) and stroma (grey). The right panel highlights a highly proliferative neighborhood 
enriched with proliferative tumor cells expressing Ki67 alone (light red) or co -expressing Ki67 and pRb 
(dark red). Undefined cells are shown in light grey. 
Analysis of the relative proportions and absolute counts of Tumor pH3+, Tumor Ki67 +, 
and Tumor Ki67 +pRb + cells revealed statistically significant differences across the 
defined groups (Fig. 15) . Notably, d espite global classification across multiple 
categories , these specific subpopulations were consistently co -enriched within the 
niche type . This indicates the spatial co -occurrence of actively proliferating cells, 
defining the highly proliferative tumor niches in post -NACT HGSC.

proliferative tumor cells within highly proliferative (red) versus low proliferative (blue) niches. The

Individual data points represent a single ROI. Box plots indicate the median and interquartile range, with

4.4 Spatial transcriptomics confirms that highly proliferative niches are 
governed by coordinated transcriptional programs 
Due to the highly proliferative tumor states observed in our proteomics analysis, we 
next set out to investigate the underlying transcriptional profiles of these tumor niches. 
Integration of spatial transcriptomic s data confirm ed that these niches are driven by 
coordinated gene expression programs. 
4.4.1 Proliferative niches effectively display transcriptional upregulation of canonical 
proliferation genes

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
t these niches are driven by 
coordinated gene expression programs. 
4.4.1 Proliferative niches effectively display transcriptional upregulation of canonical 
proliferation genes 
Tumor n iches classification with proteomics was validated through the expression 
analysis of established proliferative marker genes (Fig . 16A). Mann -Whitney U tests 
demonstrated that AOIs classified as highly proliferative showed significantly higher 
expression of core proliferative marker genes compar ed to low. Specifically, MKI67 , 
TOP2A , CDK1 , CENPF , UBE2C , CDC20, PCNA , and STMN1 showed significant up-
regulation within highly proliferative niches . These findings prove a consistency 
between the protein -level selection criteria and the underlying RNA expression profiles 
across the cohort.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
46 
 
 4.4.2 Global transcriptional partitioning with machine learning shows disparate 
biological states 
To identify the most robust transcriptional drivers of these environments, we utilized 
ssGSEA scores . Unlike standard GSEA, which evaluates pathway enrichment between 
predefined population cohorts, ssGSEA computes an independent pathway 
enrichment score for each AOI. We then applied an RF classifier to rank ssGSEA 
pathway importance. The most influential features (n=3100) were selected for 
unsupervised hierarchical clustering (Fig . 16B). This high -dimensional feature 
selection with R F classifie r ensured that the clustering was based on the most 
discriminative biological signals . 
The resulting dendrogram successfully separated the dataset into three dominant 
transcriptional branches , effectively capturing high - and low - proliferati on signatures . 
Interestingly, w hile the functional state of the niche acts as a primary driver of 
transcriptional similarity, inter -patient heterogeneity remains a significant factor in 
global architecture. Specifically, the proliferative sub -clusters were composed 
predominantly of AOIs from the same patient. This indicates that core proliferative 
programs are conserved across the cohort . Importantly , the global transcriptome is 
simultaneously shaped by the un ique genetic and environmental background of each

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
iferative 
programs are conserved across the cohort . Importantly , the global transcriptome is 
simultaneously shaped by the un ique genetic and environmental background of each 
patient.

comparing VST -normalized expression of core proliferative signature genes between proteomically -
defined Highly Proliferative (firebrick, n= 97) and Low Proliferative (royalblue, n= 96) niches. Each point 
represents a n AOI. Highly Proliferative niches exhibit significantly higher expression across all validated 
markers ( MKI67 , TOP2A , CDK1 , UBE2C , CENPF , CDC20 , PCNA , STMN1 ), confirming agreement 
between proteomic niche d efinition and RNA expression profiles. Mann -Whitney U test p -values are 
denoted as: ** P < 0.01; *** P < 0.001. (B) Unsupervised hierarchical clustering (complete -linkage, 
correlation metric) of ssGSEA enrichment scores for the top pathways (n=3100) identifi ed by R F feature 
selection. The primary dendrogram split is driven by functional proliferative state rather than patient 
factor , as highlighted by the mixing of patient IDs in the Patient annotation bar within each main cluster. 
The heat map scale bar re presents Z -score -normalized ssGSEA enrichment values .

 4.5 Differential transcriptomic profiling reveals an upregulation of amino acid 
and carbohydrate metabolism alongside mitotic machinery in highly 
proliferative nich es 
We next s ough t to identify the specific differentially expressed genes and pathways 
driving these functional differences. Differential analysis of transcriptomic profiles 
between high - and low -proliferation niches revealed significant divergence at both the 
gene and pathway levels. Statistical significance was assess ed using a LMM to account 
for multiple AOIs per sample. 
To determine the functional landscape of these regions, single -sample Gene Set 
Enrichment Analysis (ssGSEA) scores were compared across spatial niches 
(Differential Enrichment Score > 0.02, P < 0. 01) (Fig. 17). Highly proliferati ve niches 
were characterized by a characteristic transcriptional signature displaying metabolic 
reprogramming, cell cycle processes , and neuro -specific signaling. As expected for 
rapidly dividing cell populations, there was enrichment in structural and mitotic 
pathways, including MEIOTIC CHROMOSOME SEPARATION, MICROTUBULE 
DEPOLYMERIZATION, and PROTEIN LOCALIZATION TO CONDENSED 
CHROMOS OMES, alongside mechanisms of DNA replication like DNA STRAND 
ELONGATION. To meet the energetic and biosynthetic demands of this rapid division,

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
CALIZATION TO CONDENSED 
CHROMOS OMES, alongside mechanisms of DNA replication like DNA STRAND 
ELONGATION. To meet the energetic and biosynthetic demands of this rapid division, 
these niches exhibited significant metabolic shifts; GLYCINE METABOLIC PROCESS 
emerged as the most significantl y enriched pathway, supported by the upregulation of 
ASPARTATE and GALACTOSE metabolic processes. Interestingly, the analysis also 
revealed a strong signature of neuro -associated pathways, including REGULATION

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
49 
 
 OF NEUROTRANSMITTER RECEPTOR LOCALIZATION TO POSTSYNAPTIC 
SPECIALIZATION MEMBRANE and CHOLINE TRANSPORT . 
 
Figure 17. Differential Pathway Enrichment in High ly Proliferati ve Niches. ssGSEA significantly 
upregulated pathways (P < 0.01) in highly proliferative tumor regions. Bar length indicates the 
enrichment score , and color intensity corresponds to the P -value. 
Gene -level analysis identified a set of DEGs distinguishing the spatial niches (|log2 fold 
change| > 0.3, P < 0.001) (Fig. 18). Within the high -proliferation niches, FGD5 , a gene 
involved in cytoskeleton remodeling and stem -like signaling , exhibited the most 
profound upregulation, representing the highest level of statistical significance in the 
dataset ( -log10 P > 5.5). Additional top -ranked genes significantly upregulated in these 
highly proliferative regions included SOX13 , a transcription factor known to drive 
tumor plasticity and, notably, CKAP5 , which plays a crucial role in microtubule 
dynamics and mitotic spindle organization.

Figure 18. Volcano Plot of Differential Gene Expression in HGSC Proliferation Niches. Comparison of 
gene expression between highly and low proliferative niches. Significant genes (log2 FC > 0.3, P < 
0.001) are colored red (up -regulated in high proliferation) and blue (down -regulated), with top candidate 
genes labeled. Non -significant genes are shown in light gray. 
The low-proliferation niches were also characterized by a set of significantly enriched 
markers, denoted by negative log2 fold changes. This signature included BST1 , GAPT , 
and MDP1 , alongside CCND1 and OLFML3 , which emerged as the most significant 
candidates (log2 FC < -0.4). The enrichment of CCND1 (a key cell cycle regulator) and 
OLFML3 (a matrix -associated protein) suggests these regions maintain a characteristic 
regulatory state and interaction with the local microenvironment. 
Ultimately, transcriptional profiling reveal ed that high -proliferation regions are driven 
by the localized upregulation of metabolic processes , stem -like signaling , and mitotic 
machinery. 
4.6 Highly proliferative niches employ coordinated mechanisms for replicative 
stress survival and immune evasio n 
To identify the biological processes driving the specific phenotypes of the identified 
niches , GSEA was performed on the VST -transformed RNA expression data with the 
C2 Reactome pathway collection .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ical processes driving the specific phenotypes of the identified 
niches , GSEA was performed on the VST -transformed RNA expression data with the 
C2 Reactome pathway collection . High -proliferation niches revealed upregulation of

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
51 
 
 pathways cent ered on cell cycle control , genomic maintenance, and oncogenic 
signaling (Fig. 1 9). 
 
 
Figure 19. GSEA of Proliferative Niches in HGSC. Dot plot displaying top 25 enriched C2 Reactome 
pathways sorted by statistical significance (FDR q -value). The x -axis represents the Normalized 
Enrichment Score (NES), indicating the magnitude of enrichment. Bubble size corresponds to the 
percentage of genes in the leading edge. Darker red indi cates higher statistical significance (lower FDR 
q-value). 
Analysis of the high -proliferative niches revealed significant enrichment in the core 
machinery required for cell division . As illustrated in Figure 18, pathways associated 
with the M -Phase and cell cycle checkpoints were highly upregulated ( M-phase, Cell 
Cycle Checkpoints , Mitotic Prophase ). Reflecting the high proliferation rates and 
immense synthetic requirements of these niches, gene sets involved in DNA 
metabolism , specifically DNA Replication and Synthesis of DNA , were enriched. 
Genomic maintenance and stress response mechanisms were also upregulated . These 
included pathways ruling the physical management of chromosomes during mitosis, 
such as Chromosome Maintenance and the Deposition of new CENPA -containing 
nucleosomes at the centromere. Moreover, signatures of DNA repair and fork stability

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
during mitosis, 
such as Chromosome Maintenance and the Deposition of new CENPA -containing 
nucleosomes at the centromere. Moreover, signatures of DNA repair and fork stability 
were prominent , highlighted by the enrichment of Base Excision Repair , Oxidative 
Stress -Induced Senescence , and the Sumoylation of DNA Replication Proteins . By 
utilizing sumoylation, a process that tags proteins with Small Ubiquitin -like Modifiers,

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
52 
 
 these highly stressed, highly proliferative cells likely recruit repair enzymes to stabilize 
replication forks. 
Beyond the pathways aiding in maint enance of cell division and proliferation, GSEA 
identified specific signaling cascades enriched within these niches. TCF -dependent 
signaling in response to Wnt and the formation of the -catenin/TCF transactivating 
comp lex, a master oncogenic cascade, was highly significant (FDR q-value= 0.006 ), 
Metabolic adjustments were also noted , specifically the enrichment of RNA 
Polymerase I Promoter Escape , which points to the upregulation of ribosome 
biogenesis to meet the high protein synthesis needs of dividing cells. Simultaneously , 
the enrichment of the Regulation of the PTEN gene transcription point s to the active 
modulation of the tumor suppressor network . 
Finally, highly proliferati ve niches were characterized by specific regulatory signatures. 
This included the Regulation of PD -L1 Transcription . PD -L1 is a critical driver of 
immune evasion . Interestingly, these niches also exhibited upregulation of pathways 
related to viral metabolism (interactions of V pr with host cellular proteins, HCMV 
infection, and nuclear import of Rev protein), which , in the context of a tumor , may 
reflect active use of transcriptional and translational machinery .

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
53 
 
 5 Discussion 
5.1 Defining the highly proliferative “ subsister ” niches via spatial multi-omics 
The clinical management of HGSC is characterized by a high initial response rate 
followed by a common recurrence, a phenomenon often attributed to the survival of 
chemotherapy -resistant cell populations. While the presence of residual disease post -
NACT is a known prognostic factor, the molecular characteristics of the specific cells 
that survive first -line chemotherapy selective pressure remain poorly defined. In this 
study , we have explored a subset of aggressive tumor cells that survive first -line 
chemotherapy and have the potential to fuel relapse . Utilizing a spatial multi -omics 
framework , we mapped and characterize d these survival hubs, highly proliferative 
niches , which , as we hypothesize, represent the aggressive population of 
“subsister“cells . Characterizing the underlying tran scriptomics mechanisms of these 
niches is crucial for un derstanding the biological programs enabl ing chemotherapy 
subsistence. Ultimately, understanding these adaptations provides the knowledge 
necessary to design novel targeted treatment strategies. 
5.2 Conserved architecture of proliferation and stress survival 
Unsupervised hierarchical clustering of over 3,000 transcriptional ssGSEA pathway s,

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
geted treatment strategies. 
5.2 Conserved architecture of proliferation and stress survival 
Unsupervised hierarchical clustering of over 3,000 transcriptional ssGSEA pathway s, 
preselected by RF c lassifier , revealed that niches group primarily by their functional 
state ( highly vs. low proliferative) r ather than by patient identity . This is a relevant 
finding, since HGSC is characterized by great patient heterogeneity and genomic 
diversity . Our results suggest that, even though the evolutionary paths of HGSC tumors 
differ, biological adaptations to chemotherapy may be shared across individuals. 
Therefore, the fact that highly proliferative niches in HGSC exhibit a common 
transcriptional program is promising for the development of novel targeted treatments 
and patient stratification approaches . 
To bettter understand these patterns of adaptation to chemotherapy , we first examined 
the functional landscape of highly proliferative niches using ssGSEA and GSEA. 
Importantly, pathway enrichment confirmed that these cells are locked in a highly 
proliferative state, characterized by the upregulation of gene sets driving active 
progression through the cell cycle and DNA replication. 
Our analysis further characterized the basis of a coordinated adaptation program , 
which may assist tumor cells in surviving chemotherapy . First, we observed a specific

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
54 
 
 metabolic shift characterized by the enrichment of the Glycine Metabolic Process . 
Established literature identi fies glycine as a vital precursor of purine synthesis and 
glutathione production , driving rapid cancer cell proliferation and antioxidant defense 
(Amelio et al., 2014) . Therefore, its upregulation indicates that “subsister” cells 
metabolically adapt to support their high biosynthe tic demands , while managing 
oxidative stress. Crucially, o ur study adds to the current literature by demonstrating 
that this metabolic reprogramming is not a uniform tumor -wide adaptation, but rather 
a spatially restricted vulnerability within specific tumor niches. 
Furthermore, according to our findings, highly proliferative niches exhibit an 
increased capacity to withstand chemotherapy -induced genomic damage. While 
established literature demonstrates that platinum resistance in HGSC relies heavily 
on a cell’s ability to tolerate and repair DNA lesions and stabilize replication forks 
(Damia & Broggini, 2019) , our findings provide a novel spatial context for this. The 
enrichment of pathways related to DNA repair, G2/M cell cycle checkpoints, and the 
sumoylation of DNA replication proteins in highly proliferative niches points to a 
specialized spatially restricted response to severe replicative stress. The upregulation

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
the 
sumoylation of DNA replication proteins in highly proliferative niches points to a 
specialized spatially restricted response to severe replicative stress. The upregulation 
of these mechanisms suggests that highly proliferative “subsister ” cells avoid mitotic 
catastrophe by stabilizing stalled forks and managing DNA damage, allowing them to 
pass through mitosis under conditions that would be lethal to sensitive cells. These 
localized mechanisms present novel therapeutic vulnerabilities. Disrupting 
replicative fork stabilization, abrogating G2/M checkpoint , or targeting the 
sumoylation machinery may drive these surviving cell s into the cell death they 
otherwise avoid . 
5.3 Immune evasion and stem-like plasticity 
Finally, ou r analysis further revealed that “subsister ” cells actively engineer the tumor 
microenvironme nt. The targeted enrichment of Wnt/ -catenin signaling and PD -L1 
transcription pathways suggests these niches are actively evading the immune system. 
Wnt signaling is a known mediator of immune exclusio n, while PD-L1 is a primary 
drive r of T -cell inhibition (Nguyen et al., 2019) . Together, the localized activation of 
these pathways likely prevents immune cell infiltration and blocks the immune 
responses.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
55 
 
 Reflecting these broad pathway -level adaptations, our DGE analysis highlighted 
specific transcriptomics markers associated with aggressive “subsister” phenotypes . 
Most notably, we observed the upregulation of FGD5 and SOX13 , the drivers of stem -
like features in cance r cells. This observation suggests that highly proliferative niches 
maintain “stem -like” plasticity required for continu ous proliferation with in a stressful, 
post -treatment microenvironment. Furthe rmore , the downregulation of CCND1 , 
essential for G1/S transition, strongly aligns with our previous findings link ing the 
upregulation of CCND1 to better prognosis. This downregulation may indicate a “cyclin 
switch”, potentially reflecting the reliance of CCNE1 instead of ensuring rapid 
transition through the G1 phase. 
5.4 Strengths and limitations 
 
A central achievement of this study was successfully linking our prior protein -level 
explorations to deep RNA -level characterization. By integrating high -plex 
immunofluorescence (t -CyCIF) with spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx DSP), we 
effectively bridged these modalities . The primary strengths of this study lie in its cohort 
size (n=6 6), which is notably large for high -plex spatial profiling, and the successful 
multi -omics integration. By utilizing the Warpy alignment pipeline , we were able to

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
size (n=6 6), which is notably large for high -plex spatial profiling, and the successful 
multi -omics integration. By utilizing the Warpy alignment pipeline , we were able to 
bridge the 5-15 mm gap between serial sections with high fidelity , effectively mapping 
spatial transcriptomics signatures (GeoMx) onto established protein architecture (t -
CyCIF) . The high concordance between the protein -based phenotypes and the 
underlying transcriptomics signatures , specifically the significant upregulation of core 
proliferative marker genes such as MKI67, TOP2A, CDK1, CENPF, UBE2C, CDC20, 
PCNA , and STMN1 in highly proliferative niches, serves as a robust validation of our 
methodology. This approach allowed us to evaluate the post -NACT TME with a level of 
spatial precision that traditional bulk and single -cell sequencing cannot achieve. 
Furthermore, our rigorous computational pipeline combining RF classification with 
unsupervised hierarchical clustering, DGE profilin g, GSEA , and ssGSEA -based 
pathway enrichment ensures the reliability of our functional state characteri zations . 
Finally, this study possesses high clinical translation potential , establishing the 
framework for discovering actionable molecular targets within highly proliferative 
“subsister” niches.

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
56 
 
 Despite these strengths, certain limitations must be acknowledged. The inherent "z -
axis" variation between adjacent slides means that while we are analyzing the same 
niche, we are not analyzing the same cells. This may account for some of the noise in 
our hierarchical clustering , despite the strong clustering of highly proliferative AOIs . 
Furthermore , while GeoMx DSP provides deep transcriptomic data , it remains a "bulk" 
measurement within a defined area . Additionally, a limitation of this study is the 
abse nce of an independent validation cohort to confirm the identified transcriptomics 
signatures. Finally, although our analysis suggests active immune evasion, direct cell -
to-cell immune interactions within the TME were not explicitly assessed. Future 
studies using single -cell spatial transcriptomics (e.g., Xenium) will be essential to 
confirm if genes like FGD5 are expressed in highly proliferative niches and to 
accurately map tumor -immune interactions. 
Overall, t his study successfully fulfilled its aims by defining the molecular landscape of 
residual disease in HGSC. We have demonstrated tha t despite patient -specific genomic 
differences , these niches utilize a conserved transcriptomic program centered on cell 
cycle checkpoints, DNA replication, replicative stress management, and g lycine 
metabolism. 
5.5 Future perspective s

vector_chunks.source_file: Archive/Mikhailava_Nika_Mastersthesis_2026.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
tilize a conserved transcriptomic program centered on cell 
cycle checkpoints, DNA replication, replicative stress management, and g lycine 
metabolism. 
5.5 Future perspective s 
Based on these results , we think that evaluating drug resistance profiles of these niches 
via computational transcriptomics (e.g ., BeyondCell Scoring ) will help guide the 
development of targeted therapies. An important next step will be to val idate our 
findings in an independent cohort by utilizing publicly available spatial 
transcriptomics dataset s (e.g., those acquired via CosMx or Visium ) to establish the 
broader applicability of the highly proliferative “subsister” niche signature . 
Future research should prioritize functional validation in patient -derived organoids to 
determine if disrupting specific DNA repair mechanisms , cell cycle checkpoints or 
stem -like signaling can successfully collapse the “subsister ” niche s and prevent tumor 
regrowth. Ultimately, this study provides the spatial and molecular roadmap required 
to understand HGSC residual disease and transition HGSC maintenance therapy to a 
highly proliferative niche -targeted precision strategy.

 Acknowledgements 
I am very grateful to have spent 2025 –2026 working in the Färkkilä Lab at the 
University of Helsinki. 
First, I would like to sincerely thank my supervisor, Anniina Färkkilä, for her guidance 
and scientific insight. I learned a lot during your mentorship. I am especially thankful 
for the opportunity to work with spatial proteomics and transcriptomics —the 
resources and support you provided made this project possible and particularly 
meaningful to me. 
I would also like to thank my second supervisor, Ada Junquera Menc ía, for her 
constant support and for sharing her way of thinking. I have truly value d our time 
together - not only for science, but also for the way you have helped me approach things 
more strategically. Navigating these experiments and data analysis would not be 
possible without you. With you , the problem is no longer a problem. 
To the entire Färkkilä Lab team, thank you for creating such a supportive and enjoyable 
environment. I am especially grateful to Joonas Jukonen for believing in me and 
welcoming me into the lab. I would also like to thank Saundarya Shah for assistance 
with t-CyCIF staining, and Sara Palomino Echeverria for the thoughtful discussions on 
data analysis and beyond. Furthermore, I would like to thank Aleksandra Shabanova, 
Iga Niemiec, and Zhilan Liang for their work in GeoMx data acquisition and

vector_chunks.text:
ful discussions on 
data analysis and beyond. Furthermore, I would like to thank Aleksandra Shabanova, 
Iga Niemiec, and Zhilan Liang for their work in GeoMx data acquisition and 
preprocessing, a nd Elias Ruuska for developing the Warpy -based image alignment 
pipeline. 
I would also like to thank Pablo Siliceo Portugal for his positive presence in the lab over 
the past months - I truly value it. 
Finally, I am deeply thankful to my friends and family for their constant 
encouragement and support throughout the GMB master’s program. 
Helsinki, May 2026 
Nika Mikhailava

Main goals of the thesis project

Investigate the tumor immune microenvironment in HR-proficient HGSC IDS samples using Oncosys-OVA clinical trial data to analyze its composition after chemotherapy and its impact on clinical outcomes.

vector_chunks.text:
2. Criteria for calculating PFS groups

HRP 
Patients who did not receive PARP inhibitors
2 samples dropped - necrotic 
 -> 19 patients in cohort

PFS is calculated based on the following criteria:
33rd percentile distribution
Categorized into short (6), medium (6), and long (7) groups
 -> 13 patients (short and long)

vector_chunks.text:
LDA preserves spatial coherence

CD8+

CD4+

Speaker notes:
Spatial Count
Captures scale-sensitive shifts in cellular composition.
Small radius (13 µm): Detects fine, localized structures (e.g., vessels, TLS).
Larger radius (25–35 µm): Integrates into broader, heterogeneous tissue environments.
More RCN diversity, especially at small scales.
LDA
Maintains spatial coherence across radii.
Stable immune RCNs (e.g., CD4+, macrophages).
Less sensitive to scale, better at retaining fine-grained structure.
Lower RCN diversity, but more homogeneous and robust.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Presentations/Thesis_presentation_Alva_3.4.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Conclusion: No one-size-fits-all

Small radius: fine-grained, cell-level interactions
Large radius: broader tissue architectures

Aspect | Spatial Count | LDA
Sensitivity to spatial scale | Scale-dependent | Robust
Interpretation | More straight-forward | More abstract
Diversity | High | Low
Aggregation pattern | Localized | Disaggregated

PLAN ☒ 
The thesis plan is attached. 
 
The thesis plan should include 
• The title 
• Unit(s) where the experimental work will be conducted 
• Scientific background 
• Aims and objectives of the thesis 
• Thesis project description with relevant materials and methods 
• Availability of required special equipment and materials 
• Ethical considerations (necessary licenses etc.) 
• Timetable 
• References 
 
Master’s theses are public documents. A digital copy of the thesis is stored and, pending on student’s 
permission, published online. Theses for which no open publication permission has been granted, can still 
be accessed on demand. The abstract is always publishe d on line. 
 
The scope of a Master’s thesis is 30 credits, which corresponds to ca. 5 months of full -time work, including 
both research and written work. The thesis should form a coherent entity with a clear research question. 
Any materials, analyses etc. produced by others must be clearly indicated and acknowledged according to 
good scientific practice.

Processes to be targeted

- Denoising/ Background correction

- Signal enhancement filters

- Segmentation metrics

 - Intersection over union (threshold)

 - F1 score

 - Average precision

 - Precision recall curve

- Data normalization

- Compression formats

\###############################################################

Week 1: CREATING THE BENCHMARKING DATASET

- Get familiar with tCycIF image handling and markers to be utilized

- Crop relevant areas in tCycIF images - 4 (Tumor, Stroma, TLS,
 > macrophage-rich)

- Run Mesmer and Stardist on the crops to get some reference masks

- Outcome: A **dataset of annotated reference images** for
 > benchmarking.

Week 2-3: FILTER EXPLORATION AND PARAMETER TESTING

- Select Preprocessing Filters to Evaluate

 - Suggestions include:

 - Gaussian Blur, Unsharp Masking, CLAHE, Gamma correction:
 > Decide on parameters to be tested and optimized to improve
 > segmentation (over DAPI channel)

 - PENGUIN denoising: [[Welcome to PENGUIN's documentation! ---
 > PENGUIN 0.1
 > documentation]{.underline}](https://demirandalab-penguin.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)

- Create code to test on this approach and benchmark the times of
 > computation

- Outcome:

 - Computational costs (RAM, time)

 - Preprocessed images with different filtering methods

 - Initial observations on how filters/parameters affect image
 > features.

Week 4: SEGMENTATION METHODS

- Select segmentation methods to be applied (Stardist,Cellpose,Mesmer)

- Run on all filtered and unfiltered crops

- Outcome

 - Segmentation results on crops for **method combination**.

 - Image-based observations on how do these affect

 - segmentation quality

 - signal range

 - are there cell-type-specific changes?

Week 5-6: PERFORMANCE ASSESSMENT

- Select Benchmark Metrics

- Evaluate the performance based on those benchmarks

 - Compare filtering vs unfiltered images

 - Compare across distinct combinations and parameters of filtering

- Performance metrics for each **method** combination.

- Insights into which preprocessing techniques **help or hinder**
 > segmentation and phenotyping

1 
 Title 
 
Deciphering the Functional Immune Composition and Its Clinical Impact in a Cohort of HGSC 
HR- Proficient Interval Debulking Samples 
Supervisors 
 
Supervisor 1: Anniina Färkkilä, MD PhD 
Principal investigator in Färkkilä lab, Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), 
Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki. 
Supervisor 2: Ada Junquera Mencía, MSc 
Doctoral researcher in Färkkilä lab, Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), 
Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki. 
Unit 
 
Research Program in Systems Oncology (ONCOSYS), Faculty of Medicine, University of 
Helsinki 
Scientific background 
 
Ovarian cancer is one of the most prevalent gynecological cancers, known for its poor prognosis 
and high mortality rate (Momenimovahed et al., 2019) . This disease is highly heterogeneous 
and includes several histological subtypes, with high -grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) being 
the most lethal, responsible for 70 -80% of ovarian cancer deaths (Bowtell et al., 2015) . Its high 
mortality rate is primarily due to the lack of effective treatment options and the delayed 
diagnosis , often influenced by the late onset of symptoms, the tumor's asymptomatic growth, 
and insufficient screening methods (Momenimovahed et al., 2019) . 
The standard treatment for HGSC is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by adjuvant 
chemotherapy.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
c growth, 
and insufficient screening methods (Momenimovahed et al., 2019) . 
The standard treatment for HGSC is primary debulking surgery (PDS) followed by adjuvant 
chemotherapy. For patients with advanced stages of the disease who are not suitable candidates 
for PDS, the preferred option is neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) followed by interval 
debulking surgery (IDS) and adjuvant chemotherapy . (Armstrong et al., 2021) Although 
patients initially respond well to chemotherapy, the majority eventually develop resistance to 
the platinum -based chemotherapy (Böhm et al., 2016) .

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
2 
 Targeted therapies have emerged in this context as the main breakthrough within ovarian cancer 
treatment. One of the most successful has been poly (ADP -ribose) polymerase (PARP) 
inhibitors , which suppress DNA repair in cancer cells and have proven eff ective in tumors with 
pre-existing defects in the homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair pathway (Bowtell et 
al., 2015) . However, patients with HR -proficient tumors do not benefit from PARP inhibitors . 
In such cases, the primary g oal of treatment is often to manage symptoms and relieve pain to 
improve quality of life . There fore, there is a critical need to further analyze the TME in HR -
proficient tumors to better understand its characteristics and role in disease progression, 
potentially offering new treatment options for this patient subgroup . (Stiegeler et al., 2024) 
Understanding the tumor microenvironment (TME ) offers promising new targets for treating 
HGSC and potentially improving existing therapies. The TME is a complex network in which 
tumor cells interact with other cell types, such as fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and metabolites. 
The TME plays a crucial role in the development of the primary tumor and the formation of 
metastases. (Jiang et al., 2020) Immune cells within the TME have their own specific relevance, 
contributing to both immune activation and immunosuppression .

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nd the formation of 
metastases. (Jiang et al., 2020) Immune cells within the TME have their own specific relevance, 
contributing to both immune activation and immunosuppression . Tumor -infiltrating 
lymphocytes (TILs) , includ ing both T cells and B cell s, have been associated with longer 
progression -free survival (PFS) (Rodriguez et al., 2018) . In contrast , CD4+ regulatory T cells 
(Tregs) and tumor -associated macrophages (TAMs) of the M2 phenotype exhibit tumor -
promoting activity and immunosuppressive functions . The composition of immune cells within 
the TME is strongly correlated with treatment responses and overall survival . (Hao et al., 2020) 
Despite the promise of immunotherapies, they have largely failed to achieve significant results 
in ovarian cancer, partly due to the complex immunosuppressive landscape of the tumor 
immune microenvironment (TIME) (Rodriguez et al., 2018) . Moreover , NACT treatment has 
been shown to significantly influence both the immunostimulatory and immunosuppressive 
functions of the TIME, affecting both the quantity and activity of immune cells (Xue et al., 
2023) . 
Further exploration of NACT’s effects on immune cells and their functional markers , especially 
in HR proficient patient with limited treatment options , along with the integration of TME

in HR proficient patient with limited treatment options , along with the integration of TME 
analysis with genomic and molecular testing , is crucial (Jiang et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2023) .

3 
 Aims and objectives 
 
Main aim 
 
The primary objective of this master’s thesis is to investigate the tumor immune 
microenvironment (TIME) in homologous re combination (HR) proficient HGSC IDS samples 
using existing data from the ongoing Oncosys -OVA clinical trial . This analysis aims to provide 
insight s into TIME’s composition after chemotherapy and its impact on the clinical outcomes . 
Sub aims 
 
1. Refining image -derived quantified data through data normalization and assignment of 
precise phenotype labels to the cells of the TME (accounting for cell type specific 
marker intensity), also identifying the most important functional states (determined by 
Ki67, pStat1, TIM3, FOXP3, PD -1). 
2. Exploring the composition of the tumor immune microenvironment by analyzing the 
immune functional states, their relative abundance across the cohort , as well as assessing 
the cellular diversity and the possible correlations with in the cell types . 
3. Identify patient subgroups with similar immune profiles based on the samples’ 
composition analysis and study the relationship between clinical factors (PFS, PFI, 
CRS) and the distinct immune cell types and the functional states. 
Materials & methods 
 
Materials 
 
The data used in this thesis was collected from newly diagnosed patients in the Oncosys -OVA 
clinical trial.

vector_chunks.text:
types and the functional states. 
Materials & methods 
 
Materials 
 
The data used in this thesis was collected from newly diagnosed patients in the Oncosys -OVA 
clinical trial. 28 IDS samples were selected based on criteria such as progression -free survival 
of more than six months and a minimum tumor purity of 10 -15% according to an expert 
pathologist . Surgical and clinical data were gathered, and BRCA1/2 mutational profiling along 
with genomic scarring (ovaHRDscar) was conducted . This data enables a comprehensive 
assessment of chemotherapy responses, genomic status, and disease progression . 
From the same cohort , FFPE samples were collected and analyzed using tissue cyclic 
immunofluorescence (tCycIF) , a highly multiplexed immun ofluorescence imaging technique . 
In this antibody -based method, 3 -4 antibodies are added in each cycle and imaged with a highly 
reliable RareCyte CyteFinder II. Each sample underwent multiple imaging cycles with a panel 
comprising 36 different markers to characteriz e cell populations in the TME, their functional

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
4 
 states, cell cycle sta ges and DNA damage patterns . The image dataset to be utilized has already 
been processed, segmented and quantified. 
Methods 
 
This thesis is entirely a bioinformatics project, with packages in Python and R employed 
throughout its full extent. 
Normalization of data 
Normalization using state-of-the-art methods will be optimized in R . 
Cell phenotyping with scimap 
Utilizing specific bioimage analysis software called scimap in combination with Napari image -
viewer , cell type calling is performed on the images through the establishment of thresholds 
and the creation of logic tables to assign the cell labels. Phenotyp es are then identified by 
analyzing the levels of the following markers : PanCK, PAX8, CD45, CD4, CD8 a, CD20, IBA1, 
CD163, CD11c, NKG2a, Vimentin, aSMA, FAP and CD31 . 
Data analysis 
Data analysis and visualiz ation will be performed using various packages in R and Python, such 
as complexHeatmap, seaborn and pheatmap . The composition analysis will be performed using 
different types of plots such as, bar plots, heatmaps , UMAPs and box plots . The clinical data 
will then be correlated to the different subgroups of immune cells and the results from the 
composition analysis . 
Availability of required special equipment and materials

a 
will then be correlated to the different subgroups of immune cells and the results from the 
composition analysis . 
Availability of required special equipment and materials 
 
All computational infrastructure required for this thesis will be provided by Färkkilä lab. No 
biological specimens are directly handled by the student and the clinical data will be stored 
under the highest security standards as specified in the section below. 
Ethical considerations 
 
All data used in this thesis originate s from the prospective Oncosys -Ova study and has been 
approved by the ethics committees of the Women’s Clinic, Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) 
and Helsinki Biobank .

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4 analysis files/3.Alvas_MSc/Thesis plan and supervision plan/Thesis_plan_alva_gronholm_23.8.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
5 
 Patient IDs and other personal information in the clinical data will be handled with the highest 
security and confidentiality on the HUH server, using the HUH Datalake at the Helsinki 
Biobank. Access to clinical data is strictly restricted to authorized personnel for retrieval and 
use. In publicly available data, each patient will be assigned a unique, randomized ID to prevent 
any risk of patient identification. 
Timetable 
 
This project will be carried out between August 2024 and March 2025 (Figure 1) and is divided 
into three aims, detailed in the sub -aims section. I will work part-time (80%) througho ut the 
thesis work . The project will start in August 2024 with Aim 1, which focuses on data 
refinement . Aim 2, involving composition analysis of the TME , is planned for September and 
October . Aim 3, investigating correlations between the composition analysis and clinical data, 
is expected to start in Novemb er and be completed by January . The thesis writing is planned 
for two months , from January to March 2025 . 
 
Figure 1. Timeline for master's thesis project. Figure created in Canva. 
Supervision 
 
I will receive ongoing guidance and feedback from my supervisors throughout the project. 
Regular meetings will be held to review progress, discuss results, and adjust the project's 
direction as needed to achieve the research goals.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/1. t-CycIF/4_4 - PRITTI cohort experiment/4_4-experimental_files/3rd round experiment + validation of CCNE/CCNE validation results _2_3_4 cell cycle.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Order and results of the conducted experiments

Validation with Kauppi lab alliquote 1:1000 -> correct staining, we then ordered this vial
Exp2, full slide set, CCNE 1:1000 + pChk1 1:200 -> CCNE did not show any good positive signal in nuclei, cytoplasmic signal in stroma and high background. 
We realized it is actually not the same lot number as in Kauppi lab (but same clone and concentration)
Intermediate validation: CCNE 1:1500 or CCNE1:2000. Thought to check if diluting more our ab worked-> Really good pattern, 1:2000 had too low signal, so something in between 1:1600 was preferred
Exp3, full slide set, CCNE 1:1600 + pChk1 1:200 -> Fails again, multiple bubble-like structures and extremely low signal, but different from results in Exp2, here we have some nuclear signals. It is closer to the expected staining 
Exp04, subselection of the slide set 4. Aimed to test a cross reactivity between CCNE and pChk1. Same dilutions were used (1:1600, 1:200) this time some of the slides were stained only with CCNE and some in combination -> We observed much better staining, more specific pattern and with much higher intensity, but also saw some aggregates and bubble-like structures
Additional validation with 7_01 slides from Zhihan (observe difference by using a different secondary, in this case mouse 555)

vector_chunks.text:
We found differential NL (nuclear localized) and membrane-localized
(MEM) patterns of Cyclin D1.

- [**[The cyclin D1MEM was sufficient to induce G1--S cell-cycle
 > progression, cellular proliferation, and colony
 > formation]{.underline}**](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41389-020-00266-y#:~:text=D%2Dtype%20cyclins%20bind%20and,located%20in%20the%20cytoplasmic%20membrane.)

 - The cytoplasmic sequestration of cyclin D1 is important to
 > maintain the non-proliferative state as nuclear enforced
 > expression using a nuclear-localized form of cyclin D1 forces
 > the cell into a proliferative state

 - Cyclin D1 has been identified in the cytoplasmic membrane and
 > shown to bind and regulate the function of several cytoplasmic
 > membrane-associated proteins including PACSIN II (Protein
 > kinase C and Casein kinase Substrate In Neurons protein 2)23
 > also known as syndapin), Filamin A24 and paxillin21.

 - PACSIN (involved in vesicle trafficking and endocytosis)

 - Cytoplasmic-membrane associated cyclin D1 was observed in 5
 > of 6 IBC patient samples and 2 of 17 non-IBC patient
 > samples had membrane-associated cyclin D1 (Fig. S3). All 6
 > IBC and 16 of 17 non-IBC patients had nuclear-localized
 > cyclin D1

 - [Cyclin D1 is known to promote cellular migration of fibroblasts
 > and mammary epithelial cells]{.mark}

vector_chunks.text:
R2: Make a copy of the template and run the functions from the top button tCycIF MagicGUI
R3: You should give permits for the automation (Google will ask you to authenticate just once)
R4: FOR RESEARCHERS
R5: 📋 Sample_Layout
R6: Panel refers to a specific combination of antibodies used in the full experiment (e.g., A, B, C...).
R7: Versions within each panel indicate the sample split by dilution used (e.g., 1, 2…).
R8: For real experiments (not validations): | → Assign Panel A | → Use Dilution 1 only.
R10: 📋 Panel_Builder
R11: Start by launching the Panel Design Wizard in the t-CyCIF MagicGui.
R12: This links to the most recent version of the Antibody Database
R13: | Use the dropdown or input an antibody ID to auto-fill details.
R14: | Formatting (e.g., colors) will apply a few seconds after selection.
R15: You MUST fill the validation dillution column/s, for version 1 just fill down ValidationDillution1, for v2, ValidationDillution2 ...
R16: | If you are running a real experiment, not a validation,just fill ValidationDillution1 column, you should copy the ValidationDillutionLola for those antibodies that were validated
R17: | ⚠️ IMPORTANT: If an antibody is validated but missing, update the antibody database, not just your local sheet. After updating the database, run the panel design wizard again to link the new version
R18: You MUST fill the cycle number and the panel.

body database, not just your local sheet. After updating the database, run the panel design wizard again to link the new version
R18: You MUST fill the cycle number and the panel.
R19: If an antibody vial is missing you will be notified
R20: 📊 Panel_summary
R21: For presentations, publications, or organization, run 📊 Display Panel_summary in tCyCIF MagicGui.
R22: This will display a new tab per each panel, with format, cycle number, marker number in the final image and all kinds of details
R23: This is useful for later computational steps
R25: FOR RESEARCHERS
R26: 🧪 Pipetting_Calculator
R27: You can adjust per-slide volume and Hoechst dilution for automatic calculations.
R28: 🧪 RunPipettingSteps
R29: If you go to the cycif gui and then runpipettingsteps you will get
R30: 🧪 TubePrepAuto | Antibodies + exact volumes to pipet, organized by vial - set of slides
R31: 🧪 ReagentList | List of required antibodies (calculates total volume needed + locations) | | → Useful for verifying inventory and ordering.
R32: 🧪 TubePrepAuto | By default Slide_Number is displaye in Tube prep auto plan.. To change this setting to Unique_Section_ID, go to extensions->App scripts->tCycIF pipetting plan automation->generatePipettingPlan.gs in row 22 Slide_number to Unique_Section_ID.Remember to click the saving disk on top

# **Research Progress** 

**Project Title: ProteoPicking\
Student Name(s): Lina Maltrovsky\
Supervisor/Lab: Ada Junquera\
Start Date: 09.09.24\
Expected Completion Date: 31.03.25**

**\
GitHub Repository (if applicable):**\
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/ProteoPick_LinasThesis]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/ProteoPick_LinasThesis)

**Shared Folder Link:**
[[https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1FikgMgnoSuW8eaZljcNtUKKxV28b12tn?usp=sharing]{.underline}](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1FikgMgnoSuW8eaZljcNtUKKxV28b12tn?usp=sharing)

## **Project Overview**

**Brief Description of Research Goals:\
**In this project, we want to establish a protocol for a new method that
combines highly multiplexed imaging with proteomics analysis. For this
we are using the picking function of the RarecyteFinder to isolate
specific parts of the t-CycIF stained tissue. The cell samples are then
sent to a collaboration partner for the proteomics analysis.

Moreover, an analysis pipeline for the analysis of the data that is
collected during ProteoPicking experiments was created during this
project. It analyses image data & proteomics data and in the integrates
results from both parts of the project

Image Analysis:

\- Illumination Correction

\- Ashlar Stitching

\- Segmentation (Stardist, Mesmer, Cellpose; different image filters)

\- Quantification

\- Phenotyping

\- Proteomicsdata exploration

\- Differential Expression Analysis

Integration of Image and Proteomics Results

vector_chunks.text:
Ovarian Cancer

8th most common cancer for women
HGSC most lethal subtype
Limited treatment options
  primary/interval debulking surgery
  (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy
  PARP inhibitors
Low response rates for immune checkpoint inhibitors

15

Speaker notes:
Ovarian Cancer – why is it relevant?

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_MastersPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Tumor Microenvironment

promotes
 Tumor survival
 Tumor development
 Metastasis
 Immune evasion

 interesting therapeutic target

16

Speaker notes:
for an integrated spatial and proteomic exploration of the TME

Tumor microenvironment 
- heterologous environment 
plays a vital role within tumor survival, development, metastasis, immune evasion
And is therefore an interesting therapeutic target
Exploration necessary, since understanding mechanisms and interactions of tumor with its surrounding cells and how tumor recruits those cells could help to find new treatment approaches

That’s why:
Spatial resolution methods are crucial in tumor research as they enable the precise visualization and analysis of the tumor microenvironment (TME), revealing the spatial organization of tumor cells, immune cells, stromal components, and extracellular factors. These techniques help uncover key interactions driving tumor progression, metastasis, and immune evasion, ultimately guiding the development of targeted therapies and personalized treatment strategies.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_MastersPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Spatial-resolution Methods

Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based imaging methods  high multiplexing, expensive equipment, complex, time consuming

Fluorescent-based imaging methods limited multiplexing, cheap, AB-panel based, fast

17

Speaker notes:
1. Mass Cytometry/Spectrometry-Based Imaging
These include imaging mass cytometry (IMC), multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI), and related mass spectrometry-based imaging methods.
2. Fluorescent Staining-Based Imaging
These include standard immunofluorescence (IF), confocal microscopy, STED, and multiplexed fluorescence imaging (e.g., CODEX, IBEX, CyCIF).
  method is lacking for dissection of specific regions of interest and getting spatial and full proteomics insight of small regions in an affordable, customizable, simple way

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_MastersPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Problem: 
no method that provides spatial & FULL proteomic insight for small regions
 simple, affordable & customizable

Why is it needed?

Tumor microenvironment (TME)  pro-tumor environment  therapeutic target
Spatial technologies – spatial organization

18

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_MastersPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Resources of the Lab

Collection of >500 clinical ovarian cancer samples 
Highly multiplexed tissue-based Cyclic ImmunoFluorescence (tCycIF) imaging
Rarecyte CyteFinder II imaging instrument
Established computational infrastructure & image processing pipeline

19

Speaker notes:
Collection of clinical ovarian cancer samples which are collected as part of the ONCOSYS-OVA at the Helsinki Hospital  formalin fixed, paraffin embedded tissue samples
tCycIF: immuno staining + DNA dye, imaging, fluorophore bleaching
Rarecyte CyteFinder II = high-speed, whole slide imaging systems with options for multiplexed liquid biopsy and tissue spatial analysis
Computational infrastructure & imaging pipeline for image processing (illumination correction, stitching, segmentation, quanitification for single cell data, phenotyping)

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_MastersPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
ProteoPicking

combination of t-CycIF and MS-based proteomics analysis
dissection of regions of interest
structural, functional and proteomic landscape
high spatial precision

20

Speaker notes:
The mass spectrometry analysis of the picked cell samples was performed by the Turku Proteomics facility, a unit of the Turku Bioscience Centre, University of Turku.The samples were analysed using a bottom-up approach, following the facility’s established protocols. The used method was data independent acquisition shot gun proteomics via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS).

the advantages of t-CycIF and MS-based proteomics . This 
method has the potential to unveil structural, functional, and 
PTM proteomic landscapes within the tumor microenvironment 
with an unprecedented spatial precision .

Theoretical Background

for an integrated spatial and proteomic exploration of the TME

vector_chunks.text:
Ovarian Cancer

‹#›

8th most common cancer for women1
Most lethal subtype: high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) 2
Limited treatment options
 🡪 Primary debulking surgery + adjuvant chemotherapy 3
 🡪 Neoadjuvant chemotherapy + interval debulking surgery 3
 🡪 Targeted therapy4
High recurrence rate ~75% 5

Speaker notes:
Ovarian Cancer
18th most common cancer worldwide & 8th most common cancer for women
Various histological subtypes with epithelial OC being 90% of all emerging cases
Epithelial OC originates from epithelial cells
Epithelial OC has subcategories: low grade serous ovarian carcinoma, mucinous carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, endometrioid carcinoma (type 1 cancers – low grade, slowly proliferating, genetically stable) & high grade serous ovarian carcinoma (type 2 cancer – genetically unstable, aggressive, high grade)
HGSC most common and lethal subtype of OC – 70% of all OC related deaths, 
HGSC – originates from epithelium of distal fallopian tube - heterogeneous - solid cellmass, slit like fenestrations, necrotic areas, glandular/cribriform/papillary architecture - nuclear atypia, high mitotic index - high genetic instability (genome duplications, high frequency somatic copy number alterations, typically mutations in TP53 and homologous DNA repair pathways (BRCA ½), few other recurrent mutations)

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_FinalPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
ility (genome duplications, high frequency somatic copy number alterations, typically mutations in TP53 and homologous DNA repair pathways (BRCA ½), few other recurrent mutations)
No effective screening strategy 🡪 late diagnosis in stage III/IV – 5-year relative survival rate ~30% (stage III = cancer spread in ovaries, pelvic region (omentum), lymph nodes (+spread to more distant sites = IV))
Clinical treatment: 2 standard approaches - 
Low response rates for immune checkpoint inhibitors

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_FinalPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Tumor Microenvironment

‹#›

Complex, heterogeneous ecosystem 6
Important role in:
 🡪 Tumor development & survival
 🡪 Metastasis
 🡪 Immune evasion 
 🡪 Patient’s treatment response 7

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_FinalPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Spatial Resolution Methodologies

‹#›

Highly multiplexed imaging 8, 9
 = simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers in one sample 
 🡪 Sub-cellular resolution
 🡪 Limitations: limited multiplexing
Spatially resolved proteomics 10
 = spatial tissue analysis + proteomics analysis
 🡪 Limitations: scalability, sensitivity & workflow complexity

Spatial resolution = structural distinction of tissue features
j j

Speaker notes:
For structural, functional, and molecular tissue analysis important for tumor and tumor microenvironment exploration


Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based imaging 🡪 high sensitivity, expensive materials, smaller areas & low throughput

Fluorescence-based imaging 🡪 limited multiplexing, cheaper materials, large areas & high throughput 

All in all, spatially resolved proteomics approaches provide the possibility to analyze proteins within their cellular and tissue context. Thereby, they enable investigation of complex tissues, such as the TME, with high spatial precision and sensitivity, creating functional protein landscapes. However, despite their promising nature, current spatial proteomics methods face limitations in scalability, sensitivity, and workflow complexity.
While some achieve relatively high spatial resolution, none meet the precision of spatially resolved imaging techniques.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Presentations/ProteoPicking_FinalPresentation.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
ons in scalability, sensitivity, and workflow complexity.
While some achieve relatively high spatial resolution, none meet the precision of spatially resolved imaging techniques.

there remains a clear need for a spatial proteomics method with high spatial precision, simple protocols, and affordable materials. Addressing this gap will improve our ability to map the proteomics architecture of tumors, paving the way for better-targeted therapies.

New spatially resolved proteomics method

Structural, functional & molecular landscape of ROIs

Resources of the Lab

Collection of >500 clinical ovarian cancer samples 
Highly multiplexed tissue-based Cyclic ImmunoFluorescence (tCycIF) imaging
Rarecyte CyteFinder II imaging instrument
Established computational infrastructure & image processing pipeline

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Collection of clinical ovarian cancer samples which are collected as part of the ONCOSYS-OVA at the Helsinki Hospital 🡪 formalin fixed, paraffin embedded tissue samples
tCycIF: immuno staining + DNA dye, imaging, fluorophore bleaching
Rarecyte CyteFinder II = high-speed, whole slide imaging systems with options for multiplexed liquid biopsy and tissue spatial analysis
Computational infrastructure & imaging pipeline for image processing (illumination correction, stitching, segmentation, quanitification for single cell data, phenotyping)

**How to set up Cellpose** (for Windows)

1. Open Anaconda prompt

2. Create Environment:

conda create -n cellpose_env python=3.8\
(Cellpose works with Python 3.8+)

3. Activate Environment:\
 conda activate cellpose_env

4. Install packages :

pip install cellpose\
(should automatically include numpy, scipy, tiffile, pytorch, pyqtgraph,
PyQt5 or alternatives like pyside or PyQt6, numba, natsort, fastremap,
roifile, superqt)
