vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
[Sample sectioning]{.underline}

Tissue samples were processed into paraffin blocks by the tissue
preparation and histochemistry unit (TPHU) of the Anatomy Department in
Biomedicum. Of those formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) blocks 10
µm thick tissue sections were sliced with a Microm HM 355 rotary
microtome, placed on SuperFrost micros 10cope glass slides and dried by
first baking them in the oven at 56°C for 2 h for and then leaving them
at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards they were stored at 4°C until
further usage.

[tCycIF]{.underline}

*Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre-staining*

First, slides were baked at 56°C for 30 min to ensure the tissue was
properly attached to the slide. Then, all slides were deparaffinized and
rehydrated by placing them first three times in xylene for 5 min each
followed by immersing them for 3 min each in a series of ethanol
solution with gradually decreasing ethanol concentration (100%, 90%,
70%, 50%, and 30%). Finally, the slides were washed twice for 1 min in
1x Tris-buffered saline (TBS) buffer (50 mM Tris-Cl, pH 7.6; 150 mM
NaCl).

In a next step, the slides were submerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid
buffer, pH 6.0, and heated up to 95°C for 15 min in an antigen retrieval
chamber with a pressure cooker. Afterwards, the slides in citric acid

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
ubmerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid
buffer, pH 6.0, and heated up to 95°C for 15 min in an antigen retrieval
chamber with a pressure cooker. Afterwards, the slides in citric acid
buffer were taken out of the cooker to cool down for 20 min at room
temperature and then they were washed three times for 1 min each in 1x
TBS buffer. This is done to fully retrieve the antigen molecules, which
can be slightly modified and cross-linked after the formalin fixation.

To minimize non-specific antibody binding of the tissue, 200-300 µL of
Odyssey blocking buffer were pipetted onto the tissue samples, which
were then incubated for 30 min in a moist incubation chamber to prevent
evaporation and dehydration of the tissue. Afterwards the Odyssey
blocking buffer was removed by vertically tapping the slides on a tissue
paper. Then the slides were washed three times for 3 min in 1x TBS
buffer.

*Antibody staining*

Before the actual staining was performed an antibody panel was designed
that could be applied in three cycles. The antibodies were selected to
detect tumor, stromal and immune cells as well as different markers of
the cell cycle.

For each cycle of staining, an antibody mastermix was prepared
containing all antibodies for one cycle and HOECHST diluted accordingly
in Odyssey blocking buffer. Per slide 100 µL of the antibody mixture

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
ng, an antibody mastermix was prepared
containing all antibodies for one cycle and HOECHST diluted accordingly
in Odyssey blocking buffer. Per slide 100 µL of the antibody mixture
were pipetted onto the tissue sample and the slides were incubated in a
moist incubation chamber at 4°C overnight. Since only dye-conjugated
antibodies were chosen for the antibody panel no secondary antibodies
were necessary on the next day and the slides were directly washed three
times in 1x TBS for 3 min each. Afterwards, 80-100 µL of 70% glycerol
were pipetted onto the tissue samples and the slides were covered with a
coverslip for the following imaging. After the imaging the coverslip is
removed again by placing the slides vertically in 1x TBS. After a short
period of time the coverslip detached on its own and the coverless slide
was either prepared for the next staining cycle (fluorophore
inactivation) or stored in 1x TBS at 4°C for further experiments.

Two of the three tissue samples were only stained with one cycle of
antibodies (cycle 3), while the third tissue sample was stained with
three cycles.

DAPI is diluted 1:100 and when we prepare the AB mixture its again
diluted 1:100  in total 1:10000

*Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching)*

In between each staining cycle the slides must undergo fluorophore
inactivation to guarantee that no fluorescent signals from the previous
cycle remain.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
inactivation (bleaching)*

In between each staining cycle the slides must undergo fluorophore
inactivation to guarantee that no fluorescent signals from the previous
cycle remain. For this, the slides were placed in 50 mL freshly prepared
bleaching solution. The solution consists of 40 mL 1x TBS, 7.5 mL 30%
H~2~O~2~, and 2.5 mL 480 mM NaOH and 50 mL are enough for 7 to 8 slides.
The completely submerged slides were put between two LED lights for 90
min at room temperature. After 45 min, the bleaching solution was
replaced with fresh one. Afterwards, the slides were washed four times
in 1xTBS for 3 min each.

[Image acquisition, processing, and visualization]{.underline}

*Image acquisition*

The images of the tissue samples were acquired using a Rarecyte
CyteFinder® II high-throughput scanner. This is an automated microscope
platform for digital imaging that enables multiplexed, high resolution
tissue imaging of the whole slide. It contains a slide holder with space
for two slides and multiple microscope lenses for scanning the slides at
different magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, 40x). With its different
channels (488 nm, 555 nm, 647 nm, 750 nm) up to four antibodies can be
scanned simultaneously. Additionally, in each cycle Hoechst 33342 is
scanned at 361 nm. Hoechst is used for nuclear staining, and it is
especially important to stain and image Hoechst in each cycle for the

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
ditionally, in each cycle Hoechst 33342 is
scanned at 361 nm. Hoechst is used for nuclear staining, and it is
especially important to stain and image Hoechst in each cycle for the
image processing afterwards. The scanner is connected to a series of
software to control and perform the imaging. The RareCyte Instrument
Controller helps to operate the microscope, the CyteHub is a control
center for scanning assays and image analysis, with the CyteFinder the
scan process can be set up and monitored, and the Imager allows
real-time imaging and picking control (see chapter picking).

The first step of the imaging process was placing the covered slides in
the slide holder upside down since the microscope lenses are inverted
and positioned beneath the slide holder. Then, a scanning assay needed
to be created in CyteHub. In it all necessary parameters for the
scanning process were defined - slide type ("custom ROI"), sample type
("tissue"), illumination type ("fluorescence"), imaging settings such as
magnification type ("10x" for slides from which after the imaging cells
were picked), binning ("1", which merges pixels and reduces resolution),
and scan overlap (20 µm for the X and Y overlap), focus settings,
stitching settings for defining which channel the stitching of the
images is based on afterwards ("BV421" which is the channel of Hoechst),

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
r the X and Y overlap), focus settings,
stitching settings for defining which channel the stitching of the
images is based on afterwards ("BV421" which is the channel of Hoechst),
and scan channels, which assign the used antibodies and a suitable
exposure time to their respective channels. To test which exposure time
is appropriate for each antibody the Imager software was used
beforehand. After the assay was created, the scanning of the slides was
started using CyteFinder. The first time a slide was scanned a pre-scan
needed to be done that identified the tissue area on the slide. This
allowed then to manually adjust the scanning area and select focus
points for the area to be scanned, which is necessary for a successful
overlay of the various images from different cycles of one slide. The
chosen settings were stored in CyteHub and were applied to every future
scan of the slide.

The actual scanning process was performed automatically. For this, the
area to be scanned was divided into a grid of micro-regions, so called
"tiles", by the system. The tiles had overlapping regions with their
adjacent neighbour tiles to ensure a correct stitching of the tiles
during the image processing. The number of tiles depended on the chosen
magnification. The tiles were scanned one after another and only when
all channels of one tile were scanned, the scanning of the next tile
started.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
of tiles depended on the chosen
magnification. The tiles were scanned one after another and only when
all channels of one tile were scanned, the scanning of the next tile
started. After the whole selected area was scanned the raw data was
stored as a .rcpnl file for further processing.

*Image processing*

To assemble all tiles of all channels and images of one slide, the
open-source Python package ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous
Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency Registration) was used to process the
generated .rcpnl files. Its workflow consists mainly of two steps,
stitching and registration, and is based on the nuclear staining of the
Hoechst channel, since the nuclei are objects with high contrast, well
defined positions, and clear boundaries. In the stitching step adjacent
tiles of each imaging cycle were aligned, while during the registration
tiles from different imaging cycles that cover the same region were
aligned. Running the ASHLAR programme results in the generation of an
ome.tiff file, a multichannel pyramidal stacked image consisting of
multiple layers with different resolutions. This format quickens
visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only
loading a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each
moment.

*Image visualization*

With Napari.

[Cell picking from the scanned slides]{.underline}

ROIs selection

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
ing a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each
moment.

*Image visualization*

With Napari.

[Cell picking from the scanned slides]{.underline}

ROIs selection

From the antibody-stained slides three types of cells were picked, tumor
cells in M-phase, tumor cells in S-phase, and stromal cells. The
identification of corresponding cells was based on the fluorescence
intensity of different biomarkers. Tumor cells were recognized by PanCK
and PAX8. Stroma cells were identified by the biomarker Vimentin. Tumor
cells that expressed pH3 were chosen for the M-phase cells, while cell
cycle biomarker pRb marked the S-phase cells.

For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the imaging results (ome.tiff file)
were opened in the CyteHub software in the tissue result section. By
turning on the channels of the respective biomarkers the desired cells
were identified and marked with circles with a 40 µm diameter which
corresponds to the diameter of the microneedle that was used for the
picking. The selected ROIs were stored in CyteHub as a rcpickmap file.

*Picking preparation*

First, the needle holder was prepared. Next to space for six needles it
can hold six tubes and has a built-in needle discard tray. The necessary
needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL PCR
tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
eedle discard tray. The necessary
needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL PCR
tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of
the cells from the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were
added for the collection of the picked cells. Depending on the cells
picked they either contained 20 µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris, 6 M Urea,
protease and phosphatase inhibitors, pH 7.5-8) or TFE lysis buffer ().
When everything was ready, the needle holder was placed into the right
slide holder position in the machine.

Next, the Imager software was opened, and the calibration of the needle
was performed. It allows precise control over it by aligning the actual
position (x, y) of the needle with the position displayed in the Imager
and by assuring that the tip of the needle actually touches the slide
(z) during the further picking process. For the calibration, an empty
slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide holder
position. The fingerprints helped with focusing of the image. Then a
needle was selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing
solution. Afterwards the needle was positioned above the slide for
either an automatic calibration or a manual one, where the parameters of
the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were adjusted.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
le was positioned above the slide for
either an automatic calibration or a manual one, where the parameters of
the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were adjusted.

In a last step, the created .rcpickmap file that withholds the selected
ROIs was imported into the Imager software with the Imager Main Control
together with the .metadata file of the slide.

*Picking & collection*

In a first step, the picking parameters were set, like needle type (40
µm diameter needles were used), wet and draw volume (100 nL), stage
wiggles (2), and wiggle radius (15 µm). Then the slide the cells were to
be picked from was put into the left slide holder position facing
upwards and its image was focused using the Hoechst channel.
Subsequently, the picking process was performed. It comprised of a
series of steps. The needle was flushed two to three times in the
rinsing solution, then it was filled with retrieval solution before the
needle was moved to the previously selected ROIs. There the cells were
checked again for the respective biomarkers with the various channels
prior to getting picked. After the picking, the cells were deposited in
the according collection tube. As soon as the cells were collected the
next cell picking cycle was started until all selected ROIs of one slide
were picked.

Afterwards, either a new slide was put into the slide holder and the

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Protocol_ProteoPicking_Pilot.md
vector_chunks.text:
ells were collected the
next cell picking cycle was started until all selected ROIs of one slide
were picked.

Afterwards, either a new slide was put into the slide holder and the
rinsing, retrieval, and waste solution together with the collection
tubes were exchanged or the picking process was finished by rinsing the
needle three times in milliQ water and then dropping it into the needle
discard tray.

The tissue that was left on the slide the picking was performed on was
scraped with a scalpel into a tube containing 50 µL of TFE.

Finally, all picked cell samples as well as the remaining tissue sample
were snap frozen with liquid nitrogen and stored in a --80°C freezer for
further experiments.

### Background: 

Here's a categorized list of genes for Cell Cycle Dysregulation,
Checkpoint Dysfunction, and Proliferation Markers:

1\. Cell Cycle Dysregulation

These genes are key regulators of the G1/S and G2/M transitions, often
altered in high-grade serous ovarian cancer.

- TP53: Frequently mutated; regulates cell cycle arrest.

- BRCA1/BRCA2: Implicated in homologous recombination (HR); loss
 > affects G2/M progression.

- CCNE1: Amplification disrupts G1/S transition control.

- RB1: Loss of function removes G1/S checkpoint control.

- CDKN2A/CDKN2B: Loss or mutation results in unchecked CDK4/6
 > activity.

- MYC: Amplified; drives G1/S progression.

- FOXM1: Overexpressed transcription factor critical for cell cycle
 > regulation.

2\. Checkpoint Dysfunction

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/3. iCAN_data_request_Angela/iCAN information extraction for cc.md
vector_chunks.text:
ed CDK4/6
 > activity.

- MYC: Amplified; drives G1/S progression.

- FOXM1: Overexpressed transcription factor critical for cell cycle
 > regulation.

2\. Checkpoint Dysfunction

These genes are crucial for DNA damage response and maintaining proper
cell cycle checkpoints.

- ATM/ATR: DNA damage sensors; mutations impair G1/S and G2/M
 > checkpoint activation.

- CHK1/CHK2: Mediators of checkpoint signaling; mutations lead to cell
 > cycle checkpoint bypass.

- BUB1/BUB1B: Spindle assembly checkpoint regulators; mutations result
 > in chromosomal instability.

- AURKA/AURKB: Regulate mitotic spindle formation and chromosomal
 > segregation.

- PLK1: Involved in spindle assembly and mitotic progression.

- RAD51: Key for DNA repair; dysfunction leads to checkpoint failure.

3\. Proliferation Markers

These genes are often overexpressed, driving unchecked tumor growth and
aggressive proliferation.

- FOXM1: Master regulator of mitotic genes and cell cycle-related
 > processes.

- PLK1: Promotes centrosome maturation and spindle formation;
 > overexpression supports mitosis.

- TPX2: Enhances spindle assembly; often amplified.

- MYC: Potent driver of proliferative signaling pathways

###

**GeoMX/CyCIF integration analysis - DGE, pathway enrichment**

Ki67+pRb niches counts

![](media/image1.png){width="6.267716535433071in"
height="3.736111111111111in"}

Pathways enrichment analysis for Ki67+ pRb+ niches

GSEA Hallmarks

![](media/image5.png){width="6.267716535433071in"
height="3.4722222222222223in"}

The GSEA Hallmarks dataset analysis revealed several key findings:

- **DNA Repair**: Upregulated, indicating a heightened need for DNA
 > repair mechanisms to support active progression through the S
 > phase. This is essential for cells to replicate DNA efficiently
 > during cell cycle progression.

- **MYC Targets V1 & V2**: Both pathways are upregulated, driving
 > active progression from G1 to S phase. These pathways are
 > associated with increased glucose uptake, altered oxidative
 > phosphorylation, and the promotion of cancer stem-like properties.

- **MTORC1 Signaling**: Promotes cell growth and proliferation by
 > regulating cell cycle progression. Additionally, it influences
 > glucose metabolism and immune suppression.

- **E2F Targets**: These targets are active when phosphorylated Rb
 > protein fails to inhibit E2F, further driving the transition from
 > G1 to S phase.

- **G2M Checkpoint**: Indicates activation of processes facilitating
 > the transition into the G2 phase, supporting cell cycle
 > progression.

- **G2M Checkpoint**: Indicates activation of processes facilitating

Overall, the analysis suggests active cell cycle progression,
particularly the transition from G1 to S phase, driven by various
molecular pathways involved in growth, metabolism, and DNA repair.

GSEA C6 set

Upregulation of the **Minichromosome Maintenance (MCM)** pathway
indicates active DNA replication. MCM proteins are essential for the
formation of replication forks, DNA unwinding, and initiating
replication.

**Double-stranded break repair** via break-induced replication is
activated when replication forks collapse during DNA replication,
facilitating repair mechanisms to maintain genomic integrity.

**Chromosome condensation** and **kinetochore organization** suggest
active preparation for mitosis, ensuring proper chromosome alignment and
segregation.

**K6-linked ubiquitination** regulates BRCA1/2-mediated DNA repair,

active preparation for mitosis, ensuring proper chromosome alignment and

**K6-linked ubiquitination** regulates BRCA1/2-mediated DNA repair,

division.

**CXCL5** and **CXCL8** are inflammatory chemokines involved in immune
cell recruitment, particularly neutrophils, and also promote
angiogenesis. CXCL8, in particular, is associated with immune
suppression.

**ZIC2** is a transcription factor linked to cell proliferation,
stemness, and metastasis. It helps maintain stem-like properties, making
cells more aggressive and resistant to treatment.

In **hepatocellular carcinoma**, **CXCL8** and **CXCL5** are associated
with poor progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS).

In **epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC)**, **ZIC2** expression is
specifically linked to poor prognosis.

Ca signaling as a therapeutic target

**Expansion CyCIF**

Experiment planning:

OncosysOVA IDS samples were taken. If the samples were used in GeoMX or
Xenium experiments, they were automatically included in the expansion
CyCIF. To homogenize the cohort, omental samples were chosen for the
rest of the cohort. Then, omental samples were checked for quality
through HE stainings. If the tumor purity on HE staining was more than
10% and no hemorrhage or tumor necrosis was observed, the tissue was
added to the experiment.

![](media/image11.jpg){width="5.291666666666667in"
height="5.337132545931759in"}

A total of 72 samples were included in the expanded CyCIF analysis. Of
these, 27 were previously used in the discovery CyCIF.

Tissues were sourced as follows: 47 from GeoMx's prior experiments, 8
from recent Xenium cuts, and 17 freshly cut prior to the experiment.

Regarding homologous recombination (HR) status: 11 tissues were
HR-deficient, 10 had an undefined HR status, and 51 were HR-proficient.

![Screenshot 2025-09-15 at

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/5.GeoMX/4_Nika_s_analysis_results.md
vector_chunks.text:
he experiment.

Regarding homologous recombination (HR) status: 11 tissues were
HR-deficient, 10 had an undefined HR status, and 51 were HR-proficient.

![Screenshot 2025-09-15 at
13.46.28.png](media/image10.png){width="6.267716535433071in"
height="2.9166666666666665in"}

The main objective of expanding tCyCIF is to enable deep phenotyping and
mapping of cancer cell cycle states across a larger cohort. By
increasing the patient sample size, we aim to gain a better
understanding of clinical associations and enhance phenotyping of cell
cycle states using an improved functional panel. With the extended
cohort, we will also be able to integrate multiple data modalities,
including spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA sequencing, and genomics.

Interesting top hits

DLC1 - tumor suppressor that encodes a RhoGAP protein, which inactivates RhoA/B/C GTPases. By suppressing cytoskeletal remodeling and adhesion, it reduces cell motility, proliferation, and survival signaling—thereby inhibiting cancer progression.

SIGLEC-15 - immune modulator. On macrophages/myeloid cells could suppress T cell responses. Upregulated in variety of human cancers

DDX53 - promotes stem cell-like properties, autophagy, and confers resistance to anti-cancer drugs in breast cancer cells. Involved in RNA metabolism, such as splicing, translation, and stability. May regulate cell cycle and proliferation pathways. Functions are not fully understood but linked to cancer cell survival. 


Ankasha, Sheril June et al. “Oncogenic Role of miR-200c-3p in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer Progression via Targeting the 3'-Untranslated Region of DLC1.” International journal of environmental research and public health vol. 18,11 5741. 27 May. 2021, doi:10.3390/ijerph18115741
Wang, J., Sun, J., Liu, L.N. et al. Siglec-15 as an immune suppressor and potential target for normalization cancer immunotherapy. Nat Med 25, 656–666 (2019). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41591-019-0374-x

3.1.2 Patients & specimens
All tissue samples were obtained from the ONCOSYS-OVA prospective study collec-
tion or the Helsinki University Hospital. The ethics board of the Women’s Clinic,
Helsinki University Hospital (HUH), and Helsinki Biobank have granted approval for
the use of all samples and associated clinical data in this study. Patient identifiers
and personal information are securely managed within the HUH server, utilizing the
HUH Datalake infrastructure provided by Helsinki Biobank. Only authorized person-
nel have access to retrieve clinical data. To ensure patient confidentiality in publicly
available results, each individual is assigned a randomized publication ID, reducing
the risk of identification. All used samples for this project are derived from interval
debulking surgery after NACT from the omentum of HGSC patients. Table 3 contains
an overview of all used patient samples in this project.

3.2 Methods
For this Master’s thesis project two experiments, a pilot experiment and an optim-
ization experiment, were performed. Below, the experimental procedure of both ex-
periments and any differences between pilot and optimization are described. The
changes in the protocol were necessary since the analysis of the proteomics results of
the pilot experiment showed that the samples were contaminated with keratin and a
not identified type of polymer, which interfered to a certain degree with the analysis.
Figure 1 depicts the workflow of the method.
18

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Figure 1: Schematic overview of main steps of ProteoPicking.
3.2.1 Sample sectioning
Tissue samples were processed into formalin-fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) blocks
by the tissue preparation and histochemistry unit (TPHU) of the Anatomy Depart-
ment in Biomedicum, Universty of Helsinki. Of those FFPE blocks, tissue sections
were cut with a Microm HM 355 motorized rotary microtome. For the pilot ex-
periment, 8 µm thick sections were prepared, while the sections for the optimization
experiment were 10 µm thick. The sliced sections were transferred into a waterbath
set to 40 °C to completely unfold, which takes approximately 1 min. Only then the
sections were scooped up with SuperFrost Plus Adhesion Microscope Slides, dried as
thoroughly as possible, placed onto a tray, baked at 56 °C for 2 h, and dried overnight
at roomtemperature. Afterwards the samples were stored at 4 °C until further usage.
During the optimization experiment special attention was paid on keeping everything
as dust free as possible. Gloves were used throughout the entire experiment. The
microtome, the tools for transferring the sections, work surface, and storage box of
the microscope slides were cleaned thoroughly beforehand with ethanol. The water
from the waterbath was replaced with water directly from the milliQ filtering system
and the waterbath was cleaned before the refilling.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
oroughly beforehand with ethanol. The water
from the waterbath was replaced with water directly from the milliQ filtering system
and the waterbath was cleaned before the refilling. A new package of SuperFrost
microscope microscope slides was opened to prevent contamination through earlier
experiments and the slides were stored vertically in a plastic box with lid, which was
only removed during the baking step to allow the remaining water to evaporate from
the slides. In comparison, during the pilot experiments the slides were transported
and stored on wooden trays without proper cover.
19

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
3.2.2 t-CycIF
The tCycIF protocol was adapted to be compatible with proteomic mass spectrometry
assays. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), the used buffer in the established standard
protocol, needed to be replaced. Instead tris-buffered saline (TBS) was used. Even
though, PBS and TBS differ in some aspects concerning antibody-based imaging,
their key roles are the same - maintaining the pH and osmolarity to preserve tissue
morphology and protein integrity (Visikol, 2024).
Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre-staining
First, slides were baked at 56 °C for 30 min. Then, all slides were deparaffinized and
rehydrated by placing them first three times in xylene for 5 min each followed by im-
mersing them for 3 min each in a series of ethanol solution with gradually decreasing
ethanol concentration (100%, 90%, 70%, 50%, and 30% in milliQ). Finally, the slides
were washed twice for 1 min in 1x TBS (50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.6; 150 mM NaCl). In
a next step, the slides were submerged in 225 mL 10 mM citric acid buffer (10 mM
citric acid monohydrate, pH 6.0) and heated up to 95 °C for 15 min in an Antigen
retrieval Chamber High Voltage. Afterwards, the slides in citric acid buffer were
taken out of the cooker to cool down for 20 min at room temperature and then they
were washed three times for 3 min each in 1x TBS. This is done to fully retrieve the

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
buffer were
taken out of the cooker to cool down for 20 min at room temperature and then they
were washed three times for 3 min each in 1x TBS. This is done to fully retrieve the
antigen molecules, which can be slightly modified and cross-linked after the form-
alin fixation. To minimize non-specific antibody binding of the tissue, 200-300 µL of
filtered Odyssey blocking buffer were pipetted onto the tissue samples, which were
then incubated at room temperature for 30 min in a moist Kartell Polystyrene Micro-
scope Slide Box incubation chamber to prevent evaporation and dehydration of the
tissue. Afterwards, the Odyssey blocking buffer was removed by vertically tapping the
slides on a tissue paper. The slides were then washed three times for 3 min in 1x TBS.
For the optimization experiment, close attention was again paid on keeping everything
as dust free as possible. Moreover, the use of plastic containers was avoided as much
as possible. Plastic slide holders and chambers were replaced with glass slide holders
and chambers, except for the antigen retrieval chamber that was only available in
a plastic version. The ethanol chain was freshly prepared, in contrast to the pilot
experiment where already prepared solutions were reused, and also fresh xylene was
used. Reagents and buffers were prepared in glass Schott flasks and the used water

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
to the pilot
experiment where already prepared solutions were reused, and also fresh xylene was
used. Reagents and buffers were prepared in glass Schott flasks and the used water
was acquired directly from the milliQ filtering system.
20

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Antibody staining
Before the actual staining was performed antibody panels for both experiments were
designed that could be applied in three cycles (table ??) respectively two cycles
(table ??). The antibody panels used in this project had already been validated
and established in previous experiments, making further validation not necessary.
The antibodies were selected to detect tumor, stromal and immune cells as well as
different markers of the cell cycle.
For each cycle of staining, an antibody mastermix was prepared containing all an-
tibodies for one cycle and Hoechst diluted accordingly in filtered Odyssey blocking
buffer. 100 µL of the antibody mixture were pipetted per sample, with the slides being
incubated in a moist incubation chamber at 4 °C overnight. Since only dye-conjugated
antibodies were chosen for the antibody panel no secondary antibodies were necessary
on the next day and the slides were directly washed three times in 1x TBS for 3 min
each. Afterwards, 80-100 µL of 70% glycerol in milliQ were pipetted onto the tissue
samples and the slides were covered with Epredia Menzel Microscope Coverslips for
the following imaging. After the imaging, the coverslip is removed again by placing
the slides vertically in 1x TBS. After a short period of time the coverslip detached
on its own and the coverless slide was either prepared for the next staining cycle

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
by placing
the slides vertically in 1x TBS. After a short period of time the coverslip detached
on its own and the coverless slide was either prepared for the next staining cycle
(fluorophore inactivation) or stored in 1x TBS at 4 °C for further experiments.
For the pilot project, two of the three used tissue samples were only stained with
one cycle of antibodies (cycle 3), while the third tissue sample was stained with three
cycles. During the optimization all samples were stained with two cycles of antibodies.
For the coverslipping a new package of coverslips was opened to prevent contamina-
tion through earlier experiments.
21

Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching)
In between each staining cycle the slides must undergo fluorophore inactivation to
guarantee that no fluorescent signal from the previous cycle remains. For this, the
slides were placed in 50 mL freshly prepared bleaching solution (40 mL 1x TBS, 7.5 mL
22

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
30% H 2O2, 2.5 mL 480 mM NaOH). 50 mL are sufficient for 7 to 8 slides. The com-
pletely submerged slides were put between two LED lights for 90 min at room tem-
perature. During this time, the bleaching solution was replaced two times, once after
30 min and again after 60 min, after the first staining cycle and one time after 45 min
after every following staining cycle. Afterwards, the slides were washed four times in
1xTBS for 3 min each.
During this step of the protocol, for both experiments the slides were submerged in
bleaching solution in plastic dishes because there was no glass replacement that could
have been used for the optimizing experiment.
3.2.3 Image acqisition, processing, analysis and visualization
In figure 2 an overview of image acquisition (A) and image processing (B) is depicted.
Image acquisition
The images of the tissue samples were acquired using a CyteFinder II imaging instru-
ment. This is a high-throughput automated microscope platform for digital imaging
that enables multiplexed, high-resolution tissue imaging of the whole slide. It contains
a slide holder with space for two slides and multiple microscope lenses to scan the
slides at different magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, 40x). With its different channels up to
four antibodies can be scanned simultaneously. Additionally, in each cycle Hoechst is
scanned at 395 nm.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
erent magnifications (4x, 10x, 20x, 40x). With its different channels up to
four antibodies can be scanned simultaneously. Additionally, in each cycle Hoechst is
scanned at 395 nm. Hoechst is used for nuclear staining, and it is especially important
to stain and image Hoechst in each cycle for the image processing afterwards. The
scanner is connected to a series of software to control and perform the imaging. The
RareCyte Instrument Controller helps to operate the microscope, the CyteHub is a
control center for scanning assays and image analysis, with the CyteFinder the scan
process can be set up and monitored, and the Imager allows real-time imaging and
picking control (see chapter picking).
The first step of the imaging process was placing the covered slides in the slide holder
upside down since the microscope lenses are inverted and positioned beneath the
slide holder. Then, a scanning assay needed to be created in CyteHub. In it all
necessary parameters for the scanning process were defined - slide type (“custom
ROI”), sample type (“tissue”), illumination type (“fluorescence”), imaging settings
such as magnification type (“10x” for slides from which after the imaging cells were
picked), binning (“1”, which merges pixels and reduces resolution), and scan overlap
23

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Figure 2: Overview of the image acquisition and processing workflow for multiplexed tissue
imaging. ATissue sections are imaged using a Rarecyte Cytefinder II scanner, generating
multi-channel, tiled image stacks (rcpnl files) with associated metadata and stitched images
of each cycles individually. BImages undergo several steps during image processing. Each
step can be visually assessed using Napari image viewer.
(20µm for the X and Y overlap), focus settings, stitching settings for defining which
channel the stitching of the images is based on afterwards (“BV421” which is the
channel of Hoechst), and scan channels, which assign the used antibodies and a
suitable exposure time to their respective channels (used exposure time see table
4 respectively table4). To test which exposure time is appropriate for each antibody
the Imager software was used beforehand.
After the assay was created, the slide scanning was started using CyteFinder. The
first time a slide was scanned a pre-scan needed to be done that identified the tissue
area on the slide. This allowed then to manually adjust the scanning area and select
focus points for the area to be scanned, which is necessary for a successful overlay of
24

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
the various images from different cycles of one slide. The chosen settings were stored
in CyteHub and were applied to every future scan of the slide.
The actual scanning process was performed automatically. For this, the area to be
scanned was divided into a grid of micro-regions, so called “tiles”, by the system. The
tiles had overlapping regions with their adjacent neighbour tiles to ensure a correct
stitching of the tiles during the image processing. The size and number of tiles de-
pended on the chosen magnification and are automatically calculated by the system.
The tiles were scanned one after another and only when all channels of one tile were
scanned, the scanning of the next tile started. After the whole selected area was
scanned the raw data was stored as a rcpnl file for further processing. Moreover, for
each scanning cycle an ome.tiff file was created from the CyteFinder II programme
that was used for the region of interest (ROI) selection (see Cell picking).
Image processing
The image processing mainly was conducted using parts of the already established
image processing workflow of the F¨ arkkil¨ a research group of the University of Hel-
sinki (F¨ arkki¨ a lab, 2025). Only segmentation and phenotyping were performed using
Python scripts adapted from already existing code or completely self written. These

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
y of Hel-
sinki (F¨ arkki¨ a lab, 2025). Only segmentation and phenotyping were performed using
Python scripts adapted from already existing code or completely self written. These
scripts are available on the F¨ arkkil¨ a Lab Proteopick GitHub (F¨ arkkil¨ a lab, 2025).
The workflow for processing t-CycIF images is depicted in figure ??.
Illumination of the rcpnl files generated by the CyteFinder programme is corrected
using BaSiC ( Background and Shading Correction)(Peng et al., 2017). BaSiC per-
forms flat-field estimation by modeling illumination bias, non-uniform brightness from
the light source, and shading bias, edge darkening due to vignetting or optical ab-
errations, ensuring uniform image illumination. Uneven illumination, often causing
darker tile edges, can interfere with quantitative analysis and disrupt further pro-
cessing. The correction eliminates these variations, ensuring consistent fluorescence
intensity measurements and reliable downstream analysis.
To assemble all tiles of all channels and images of one slide, the open-source Python
package ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous Harmonization of Layer/Adjacency
Registration)(Muhlich et al., 2022) was used to process the illumination corrected
rcpnl files. Its workflow consists mainly of two steps, stitching and registration, and

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
djacency
Registration)(Muhlich et al., 2022) was used to process the illumination corrected
rcpnl files. Its workflow consists mainly of two steps, stitching and registration, and
is based on the nuclear staining of the Hoechst channel, since the nuclei are objects
with high contrast, well defined positions, and clear boundaries. In the stitching
step adjacent tiles of each imaging cycle were aligned, while during the registration
25

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
tiles from different imaging cycles that cover the same region were aligned. Running
the ASHLAR programme results in the generation of an ome.tiff file, a multichannel
pyramidal stacked image consisting of multiple layers with different resolutions. This
format quickens visualization and, if necessary, further processing steps by only load-
ing a partial image with the necessary layers into memory at each moment.
After the segmentation of the images, a python script was prepared that cut out small
crops that contained the selected ROIs of the images. All further processing steps
were performed using those small crops instead of the whole slide images.
The segmentation was performed using the nuclear segmentation model of Mesmer
(Mesoscopic Segmentation of Cellular and Nuclear Regions)(Greenwald et al., 2022).
Mesmer is a suite of pre-trained deep learning models designed for high-quality bio-
logical image analysis. It uses a Convolutional Neural Network to generate per-pixel
probability values for nuclear regions, cell boundaries, and background. A distance
transform and post-processing algorithm, similar to the watershed method, refine
these probability maps to accurately segment individual nuclei, even in densely packed
regions. Prior to segmentation, gamma correction and unsharp masking, two contrast

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
d, refine
these probability maps to accurately segment individual nuclei, even in densely packed
regions. Prior to segmentation, gamma correction and unsharp masking, two contrast
and sharpness enhancement methods, were applied to enhance the visibility of nuclei
by increasing background intensity and sharpening cell edges. These adjustments
improved the performance of Mesmer in segmenting the images. Segmentation was
performed based on the Hoechst channels. The used parameters for gamma correc-
tion and unsharp masking ( γ= 1.5, σ= 1.0, strength = 2.5) were chosen based
on empirical testing. Outcome are cell masks for every image saved as ome.tif files.
The used Python script integrates code adapted from the Van Valen Lab’s DeepCell
Applications GitHub (Van Valen Lab, 2025).
Single-cell information was extracted by quantifying the data using the scikit-image
Python package (Van der Walt et al., 2014) on the generated masks, producing a
data frame with multiple parameters for spatial analysis. This data frame includes
key features such as CellID, x and y coordinates, eccentricity, and median marker
intensity values for each object across all channels.
Phenotyping
The segmented cells were phenotyped using Scimap, a scalable toolkit for analyzing
spatial molecular data. The process operates on the single-cell data frame generated
26

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
in the prior step, incorporating X and Y coordinates along with marker intensity val-
ues (Nirmal & Sorger, 2024). Gating was applied as a key step in the workflow, where
thresholds were set to categorize cells based on marker expression. A logic map, as
seen in table 6, was created to systematically specify these categories as phenotypic
classifications. All in all, this resulted in a classification of cells into specific phen-
otypes based on marker expression levels. The phenotype data was added to the
single-cell data frame.
Table 6: Logic map for phenotyping.
Iba1 CD45 PAX8 Ki67 pRb1 p21 Vimentin
all Tumor anypos neg
all Non-Tumor neg pos
Tumor T - Ki67+p21- allpos allneg
Tumor T - Ki67-p21- allneg allneg
Tumor T - p21+ anypos anypos allpos
Tumor T - Ki67-pRb-p21+ allneg allneg allpos
Non-Tumor Immune anypos anypos
Non-Tumor Stroma allneg allneg
Stroma S - p21- allneg allneg
Stroma S - p21+ anypos anypos allpos
Stroma S - Ki67-pRb-p21+ allneg allneg allpos
While the images of the pilot experiment were only stitched for visualizing the tissue
samples, the generated images of the optimization experiment were processed follow-
ing every step of the described workflow.
Image analysis
The images were analysed using the single-cell data frame that was generated during
the image processing. A Python script was created that enabled accessing the data,

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
age analysis
The images were analysed using the single-cell data frame that was generated during
the image processing. A Python script was created that enabled accessing the data,
specifying cells from selected image areas (ROIs), and performing quantitative eval-
uations (F¨ arkkil¨ a lab, 2025).
Image visualization
The open-source multi-dimensional image viewer Napari (napari contributors, 2019)
was used for visualization throughout the image processing. It facilitated the visual-
ization and combination of different channels while allowing adjustments to contrast,
27

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
intensity, opacity, and color to enhance image interpretation. Additionally, Napari
enabled the overlay of segmentation masks onto images, providing a clear depiction
of cell boundaries and aiding in quality control of the cell segmentation results. Its
interactive capabilities were particularly useful for visually inspecting and validating
each processing step, the phenotying results, and any analysis outputs ensuring the
accuracy.
3.2.4 Cell picking
ROIs selection
For selecting the ROIs on the slide, the ome.tiff files of the individual cycles were
opened in the CyteHub software in the tissue result section. The desired cells were
identified and marked with circles with a 40 µm diameter which corresponds to the
diameter of the microneedle that was used for the picking. The selected ROIs were
stored in CyteHub as a rcpickmap file.
Pricking preparations
The necessary needles were loaded into their holding spots along with three 0.2 mL
PCR tubes that contained each 200 µL 1x TBS for needle rinsing, retrieval of the
cells from the slide, and waste discarding. Additional tubes were added for the
collection of the picked cells. Depending on the cells picked they either contained
20µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 6 M Urea, pH 7.5-8, protease and phosphatase
inhibitor tablets) or 20 µL TFE lysis buffer (50% (v/v) 2,2,2-trifuoroethanol, 300 mM

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
r contained
20µL of Urea buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl, 6 M Urea, pH 7.5-8, protease and phosphatase
inhibitor tablets) or 20 µL TFE lysis buffer (50% (v/v) 2,2,2-trifuoroethanol, 300 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0, protease and phosphatase inhibitor tablets). When everything was
prepared, the retrieval module was placed into the right slide holder position in the
machine.
Next, the Imager software was opened, and the calibration of the needle was per-
formed. It allows precise control over it by aligning the actual position (x, y) of the
needle with the position displayed in the Imager and by assuring that the tip of the
needle actually touches the slide (z) during the further picking process. For the cal-
ibration, an empty slide with fingerprints on it was placed into the left slide holder
position. The fingerprints helped with focusing of the image. Then a needle was
selected, loaded, and flushed three times with the rinsing solution. Afterwards the
needle was positioned above the slide for either an automatic calibration or a manual
one, where the parameters of the needle position (x, y, z) and slide position (z) were
adjusted.
In a last step, the created rcpickmap file that withholds the selected ROIs was im-
28

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ported into the Imager software with the Imager Main Control together with the
metadata file of the slide.
Picking & collection
In a first step, the picking parameters were set, needle type (40 µm diameter needles
were used), wet and draw volume (150 nL), stage wiggles (2), wiggle radius (15 µm),
and overdrive (5 µm). Then the slide the cells were to be picked from was put into
the left slide holder position facing upwards and its image was focused using the
Hoechst channel. Subsequently, the picking process was performed. It comprised
of a series of steps, see figure 3. The needle was flushed two to three times in the
rinsing solution, then it was filled with retrieval solution before the needle was moved
to the previously selected ROI. There the cells were checked again for the respective
biomarkers with the various channels ant then the cells were picked. After the picking,
the cells were deposited in the according collection tube. As soon as the cells were
collected the next cell picking cycle was started until all selected ROIs of one slide were
picked. Afterwards, either a new slide was put into the slide holder and the rinsing,
retrieval, and waste solution together with the collection tubes were exchanged or the
picking process was finished by rinsing the needle three times in milliQ water and
then dropping it into the needle discard tray.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
er with the collection tubes were exchanged or the
picking process was finished by rinsing the needle three times in milliQ water and
then dropping it into the needle discard tray. The tissue that was left on the slide
the picking was performed on was scraped with a scalpel into a tube containing 50 µL
of TFE buffer (pilot experiment) or Urea buffer (optimization experiment). Finally,
all picked cell samples as well as the remaining tissue samples were snap frozen with
liquid nitrogen and stored in a –80 °C freezer for further experiments.
Figure 3: Visualization of the four picking steps - positioning of the needle, lowering the
needle to dissect the tissue, detaching the tissue via wiggling, and collecting the tissue.
3.2.5 Proteomics analysis
Mass spectrometry analysis
29

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/2. Proteopicks/Protocols/Materials_Methods_Pilot_and_Optimization.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
The mass spectrometry analysis of the picked cell samples was performed by the Turku
Proteomics facility, a unit of the Turku Bioscience Centre, University of Turku. The
samples were analysed using a bottom-up approach, following the facility’s established
protocols. The used method was data independent acquisition shot gun proteomics
via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS).
Proteomics data analysis

Findings

ABI3BP
Tumor suppressor in lung, thyroid, and breast cancers. 
Loss of expression linked to tumor progression and poor prognosis. 
FAM107A
Tumor suppressor frequently downregulated in gliomas and other cancers. 
No strong evidence for involvement in HGSC. 
 CD70
Overexpressed in renal cell carcinoma, glioblastoma, and hematologic malignancies. 
Facilitates immune evasion and cancer cell survival. 
NELL1
Functions vary by context: tumor suppressor in osteosarcoma, oncogenic in glioblastoma. 
Involved in cell adhesion and signaling pathways related to growth.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/5.GeoMX/Копия 21072025_GeoMX_data_initial_exploration.pptx
vector_chunks.text:
Findings

CXCL5
Type: Chemokine involved in inflammation and neutrophil recruitment. 
In Cancer: 
Promotes tumor growth, angiogenesis, and metastasis in various cancers (lung, gastric, prostate). 
Overexpressed in ovarian cancer, including HGSC, and associated with poor prognosis. 
Mechanism: Activates CXCR2 receptor, triggering MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways. 
CXCL8 (also known as IL-8)
Type: Pro-inflammatory cytokine. 
In Cancer: 
Strongly linked to tumor progression, angiogenesis, immune suppression, and chemoresistance. 
Upregulated in HGSC and associated with aggressive behavior and poor outcome. 
ZIC2 (Zic Family Member 2)
Type: Transcription factor 
In Cancer: 
Acts as an oncogene in several cancers including glioblastoma, pancreatic, and ovarian. 
Overexpression linked to proliferation, invasion, and stemness. 
Upregulated in HGSC, where it contributes to tumor cell growth and chemoresistance.

vector_chunks.text:
# LCM-Proteomics Pipeline: Troubleshooting and DVP Replication

## 1. Root Cause Analysis: Low Protein Yield and Polymer Contamination

The previous experimental run experienced significant polymer
contamination and suboptimal protein identification. When comparing the
executed workflow against the Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) pipeline,
several critical discrepancies emerge as the primary sources of these
issues:

- **Plate Material and High-Heat Decrosslinking:** Standard 4titude
 > 96-well PCR plates were utilized. These plates are manufactured
 > with plasticizers and mold-release agents. Subjecting these plates
 > to high-heat FFPE thermal decrosslinking in the presence of urea
 > aggressively leached these polymers into the microscopic sample
 > volume, causing massive mass spectrometer interference.
 > Furthermore, standard polypropylene intrinsically binds proteins,
 > leading to heavy sample loss when dealing with low-abundance
 > inputs.

- **Slide Membrane Chemistry:** The use of PET (Polyethylene
 > terephthalate) membranes rather than PEN (Polyethylene
 > naphthalate) or PPS (Polyphenylene sulfide) contributed to the
 > background.

- **Buffer Selection:** Collecting directly into 20 μL of
 > high-concentration urea is chemically aggressive. The DVP pipeline
 > utilizes Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC), which is a volatile,

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.md
vector_chunks.text:
r Selection:** Collecting directly into 20 μL of
 > high-concentration urea is chemically aggressive. The DVP pipeline
 > utilizes Ammonium Bicarbonate (ABC), which is a volatile,
 > MS-compatible buffer that evaporates cleanly during vacuum drying
 > without stripping plastics or leaving residual salts.

- [**Commercial Blocking Buffers:** The application of proprietary
 > blockers (like Odyssey) during staining introduces exogenous
 > proteins (e.g., casein, BSA) and polymers that can mask the
 > delicate endogenous peptide signals of the tissue.]{.mark}

In Lina's experiments - PCR tubes were utilized, FG-021 (Nippon
Genetics)

## 2. Protocol Comparison: Executed Experiment vs. DVP Standard

### A. Refined Summary of the Previously Performed Experiment (Helsinki Lab)

Samples were sectioned onto metal-frame PET membrane slides (pretreated
with UV-light) and baked at 60°C. Staining was performed using a custom
fluorescence panel and a commercial Odyssey blocking buffer. To minimize
tissue detachment risks, aggressive pressure-cooker antigen retrieval
was bypassed. Laser capture microdissection (LCM) was conducted at FIMM
utilizing fluorescence guidance to bypass lengthy Whole Slide Imaging
(WSI). Dissected tissue elements were catapulted directly into a
standard 4titude 96-well PCR plate pre-filled with 20 μL of urea buffer.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.md
vector_chunks.text:
nce to bypass lengthy Whole Slide Imaging
(WSI). Dissected tissue elements were catapulted directly into a
standard 4titude 96-well PCR plate pre-filled with 20 μL of urea buffer.
Following collection, the plate was snap-frozen, shipped for mass
spectrometry, and subjected to FFPE thermal decrosslinking, resulting in
extensive polymer leaching.

### B. Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) Pipeline (Lengyel Lab)

FFPE tissues are sectioned onto 1.0 PEN membrane slides pre-treated with
UV-light and VECTABOND reagent to ensure tissue adhesion during harsh
treatments. Following deparaffinization and rehydration, samples undergo
heat-induced epitope retrieval and are stained avoiding complex protein
blockers. High-resolution imaging and machine-learning-driven
segmentation define the cutting contours. The LCM catapults designated
cell populations into *dry*, empty wells of an MS-grade 384-well plate
(e.g., Eppendorf Protein LoBind). In-well lysis and thermal
decrosslinking are executed simultaneously using an Ammonium Bicarbonate
(ABC) buffer at 95°C. Digestion follows using a mixture of LysC and
Trypsin in an ABC/Acetonitrile buffer. Finally, peptides are
vacuum-dried, resuspended, and injected directly into the LC-MS via an
autosampler.

## 3. Checklist for Future DVP-Aligned Experiments

 -----------------------------------------------------------------------

autosampler.

## 4. Standard Operating Procedure: Replicating the DVP Pipeline

1. **Slide Preparation:** Treat 1.0 PEN membrane slides with VECTABOND.
 > Mount FFPE sections (5-7 μm) and bake to ensure adherence.

2. **Deparaffinization & Staining:** Deparaffinize using xylene and
 > rehydrate through graded ethanol. Perform necessary antigen
 > retrieval and immunofluorescence staining. Strictly avoid complex,
 > undefined commercial blocking agents.

3. **Laser Capture Microdissection (LCM):** Target the defined regions
 > of interest (e.g., HGSC subsister cells). Dissect and catapult the
 > tissue directly into the dry wells of an Eppendorf 384-well
 > Protein LoBind plate.

4. **In-Well Lysis and Decrosslinking:** Centrifuge the plate briefly
 > to ensure all tissue is at the bottom. Add the MS-compatible lysis
 > buffer (e.g., 20 μL of Ammonium Bicarbonate). Seal the plate
 > tightly and heat at 95°C for 60 minutes to decrosslink the FFPE
 > matrix.

5. **Enzymatic Digestion:** Cool the plate. Add the secondary digestion

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.md
vector_chunks.text:
Bicarbonate). Seal the plate
 > tightly and heat at 95°C for 60 minutes to decrosslink the FFPE
 > matrix.

5. **Enzymatic Digestion:** Cool the plate. Add the secondary digestion
 > buffer (e.g., Acetonitrile in ABC) and heat briefly at 75°C.
 > Introduce LysC and Trypsin for overnight digestion at 37°C.

6. **Quench and Dry:** Halt the digestion by adding 1% TFA. Vacuum dry
 > the plate completely to remove all volatile buffers.

7. **Mass Spec Preparation:** The dry peptides can be safely stored or
 > immediately resuspended in LC-MS loading buffer (e.g., 2%
 > Acetonitrile, 0.1% TFA) for direct autosampler injection.

### Phase 1: Sample Preparation & Collection (Helsinki Lab)

1. **Slide Prep:** FFPE tissue samples were sectioned onto **PET**
 > (Polyethylene terephthalate) metal-frame slides and baked at 60°C.

2. **Staining:** The slides were deparaffinized, rehydrated, and
 > stained using a custom fluorescence panel along with the
 > commercial **Odyssey blocking buffer**. High-heat antigen
 > retrieval was skipped to prevent tissue detachment.

3. **Dissection:** Using fluorescence-guided LCM at FIMM, specific
 > tissue regions were catapulted directly into a **standard 4titude
 > 96-well PCR plate**.

4. **Collection Medium:** The wells of the 4titude plate were
 > pre-filled with **20 µL of high-concentration urea buffer**.

5.

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.md
vector_chunks.text:
into a **standard 4titude
 > 96-well PCR plate**.

4. **Collection Medium:** The wells of the 4titude plate were
 > pre-filled with **20 µL of high-concentration urea buffer**.

5. **Shipping:** The 96-well plate was snap-frozen and shipped via UPS
 > to Otto for proteomics processing.

### Phase 2: Mass Spectrometry Processing (Otto's Lab)

1. **Thermal Decrosslinking (The primary failure point):** Because the
 > samples were FFPE, they required high heat to break the formalin
 > crosslinks. This high heat was applied to the samples *while they
 > were still in the 4titude standard PCR plates* in the presence of
 > the hot urea buffer. This aggressive combination chemically
 > leached the manufacturing plasticizers directly out of the plate
 > walls.

2. **\"One-Tip\" Protocol Application:** Otto then applied the
 > streamlined \"One-Tip\" methodology to the samples. This likely
 > involved adding a master mix containing TEAB buffer, MS-compatible
 > detergents (like DDM), and the enzymes Trypsin and Lys-C to
 > simultaneously lyse and digest the proteins.

3. **Data Acquisition:** The processed peptide mixtures were injected
 > into the LC-MS system for reading.

### Phase 3: The Results (Current Status)

1. **Severe Polymer Contamination:** The mass spectrometer readings
 > were completely overwhelmed by polymer interference. This

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/LCM-Proteomics Pipeline_ Troubleshooting and DVP Replication.md
vector_chunks.text:
ading.

### Phase 3: The Results (Current Status)

1. **Severe Polymer Contamination:** The mass spectrometer readings
 > were completely overwhelmed by polymer interference. This
 > background noise originated from two sources: the vaporized PET
 > membrane from the LCM cutting, and the leached plasticizers from
 > the 4titude plates reacting with the hot urea.

2. **Low Protein Identification:** Very few endogenous tissue proteins
 > were identified. This is due to a combination of factors:

 - The massive polymer peaks masking the delicate peptide signals.

 - Sample loss from proteins irreversibly sticking to the standard
 > polypropylene walls of the 4titude plate.

 - Potential masking from the exogenous proteins present in the
 > Odyssey blocking buffer.

vector_chunks.text:
**Student:** MSc Nika Mikhailava

**Principal Investigator:** Professor Anniina Färkkilä

**Project Title:** **Single-cell spatial proteomic profiling of
subsister cell phenotypes in HGSC**

### Introduction & Preliminary Findings

First-line therapies in high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) often fail to
completely eradicate the tumor, leaving a reservoir of cells that
survive chemotherapy - termed \"**subsister cells**\". We hypothesize
that these spatially distinct and phenotypically heterogeneous cell
populations drive divergent clinical outcomes, including tumor relapse.

To investigate this, we performed highly multiplexed **t-CycIF (40
markers, n=27)** and **Xenium spatial transcriptomics (430 genes,
n=16)** on Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS) samples. Our preliminary
spatial analysis resolved these subsister cells into phenotypically
distinct and spatially segregated niches, identifying Highly
Proliferative, CCND1-dominant, and Heterogeneous clinically relevant
patient archetypes. Crucially, the enrichment of the **Highly
Proliferative niches (Ki67+/pRb+/pH3+)** significantly correlated with
shorter Progression-Free Intervals (PFI). This finding suggests that
these specific spatial niches actively drive tumor relapse.

### **Motivation for Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)**

While our targeted approaches (t-CycIF, Xenium) successfully mapped the

vector_chunks.source_file: Data & Figures/Data/6. Deep Visual Proteomics/NikaMikhailava_InternshipObjectives.md
vector_chunks.text:
specific spatial niches actively drive tumor relapse.

### **Motivation for Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)**

While our targeted approaches (t-CycIF, Xenium) successfully mapped the
architecture of these niches, they are limited by predefined antibody
and gene panels. To discover actionable therapeutic vulnerabilities, we
need an unbiased characterization of their full proteomic landscape.
**Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP)** perfectly bridges this gap, enabling
the precise excision of these specific subsister niches to uncover their
proteome in a spatially preserved context.

### Study Cohort

DVP will be executed on FFPE tumor samples from **8-16 IDS patients**.
To evaluate the epithelial tumor compartments, we will select 3--6
regions of interest (ROIs) per tissue section, laser-microdissecting
approximately 200 tumor cells per ROI.

These samples were specifically chosen because they constitute our
\"integrated multi-omics\" cohort. By utilizing serial sections from
these exact patients, we can anchor the new DVP data directly to our
existing profiles (matched t-CycIF, Xenium, scRNA-seq, and WES). If
resources permit, spatial proteomic profiling will be expanded to
additional samples from our broader t-CycIF cohort **(up to 69 IDS
samples)**.

### Internship Aims & Tasks

**Main Aim:** **To explore the spatial proteomic landscape of clinically
relevant subsister niches.**

samples)**.

### Internship Aims & Tasks

**Main Aim:** **To explore the spatial proteomic landscape of clinically
relevant subsister niches.**

**Pre-arrival Phase (Helsinki):**

- Conduct pilot laser capture microdissection (LCM) to validate
 > protocols and assess infrastructure readiness.

- Explore clinical associations of subsister phenotypes with bulk
 > RNA-seq data.

**Internship Phase (Chicago):**

- Acquire hands-on technical proficiency in the complete DVP
 > methodology.

- Perform image-guided LCM to isolate predefined subsister niches from
 > a scalable cohort ranging from 8 (multi-omics cohort) to 69
 > (t-CycIF cohort) patient samples.

- Conduct high-sensitivity mass spectrometry to comprehensively
 > profile the proteome of these isolated regions and define
 > targetable vulnerabilities.

vector_chunks.text:
**Goal**: Spatial profiling of therapeutically targetable, cell
cycle-related molecules in Post-NACT HGSC HRP patients

Strengths:

- Cell cycle-related spatial biology has never been studied within
 > HGSC

- Many vulnerabilities (CCNE amp, DDR) are key targets that could
 > potentially succeed in the treatment

- Most of the usable markers are nuclear stainings, which eases the
 > process

- Cell fate is found within the intersection of many other processes
 > that can be also studied in the same cohort (metabolism, DNA
 > damage etc)

Potential risks of treatments of interest:

- 1\) off-target inhibitory effect on close family members of target
 > protein

- 2\) acquired drug resistance due to mutants on target or upstream
 > regulators

- 3\) many of these drugs are still under development

Technical considerations (DNA vs S phase marker, dimensionality
reduction with the cyclins?):

- System to classify arrested/cycling/proliferating cells

- Classification of cell cycle stages

- Profile immune activation/inactivation/escape/exclusion

- Non-canonical patterns need to be adressed - Ilastik?

- We are working on 2D slides (cannot quantify full cell
 > content/morphology)

![](media/image1.png){width="5.817708880139983in"
height="2.8268471128608925in"}

**Elements of interest:**

> Basic TME phenotyping + immune checkpoints
>
> Cyclins/cdk's:

ogy)

![](media/image1.png){width="5.817708880139983in"

**Elements of interest:**

> Basic TME phenotyping + immune checkpoints
>
> Cyclins/cdk's:

- **CDK4/6 Inhibitors** - I believe are promising in combination with
 > IO treatments

 - **Palbociclib, ribociclib,
 > abemaciclib**![](media/image2.png){width="2.026042213473316in"
 > height="1.7292333770778652in"}

 - Rb intact tumors are more sensitive

 - mTOR pathway can cause resistance

 - presence/accumulation of cyclin E and CDK2 are adaptations that
 > cause resistance (BC)

 - In PALOMA trials they concluded that it:

 - Causes cell cycle arrest

 - Decreases CD4+ FOXP3+ T reg/CD8+ T cell ratio

 - Increases IFN III levels and T cell activation

 - Increases PD-L1 and impairs DDR through HR

- **CCND1-related treatments**

 - GSK3b is crucial for its degradation

 - **Metformin** (AMPK activator, glycolisis inhibitor)**-** causes
 > cell cycle arrest by decreasing CCND1 levels through AMPK
 > GSKbeta levels (suppressing AKT)

 - **Mk-2206, Perfosine** Pan AKT inhibitors might work but they
 > are not clinically approved

- **CCNE**

 - There are common amplifications in these patients

 - Overexpression deregulates the cell cycle at G1/S and G2/M by
 > accelerating S phase entry, causes replication stress

 - Confers resistance to platinum therapy in HGSC

vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.md
vector_chunks.text:
HGSC is often diagnosed at advanced stages due to the lack of effective
screening strategies, together with unspecific symptomatology.
First-line chemotherapy regimens have evolved over the past three
decades. Single-agent melphalan chemotherapy was initially replaced, in
the 1970s, by the combination of **cyclophosphamide with the
anthracycline doxorubicin** and, in the 1980s, was replaced by
concurrent **cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin and cisplatin**, or
**cyclophosphamide and cisplatin only**.
[[Carboplatin]{.underline}]{.mark} was also introduced in the 1980s and
was found to have comparable efficacy to cisplatin, but with
significantly less toxicity. In the early 1990s, **paclitaxel** was
shown to be the most effective agent in patients with relapsed
platinum-refractory disease, and this established its role in the
treatment of ovarian cancer. Since then, a number of trials have
evaluated whether paclitaxel can contribute to platinum-based first-line
chemotherapy.
([[https://www.nature.com/articles/nrc1123]{.underline}](https://www.nature.com/articles/nrc1123))

[CYCLOPHOSPHAMIDE]{.underline}

- **Mechanism of action**:

 - It is a pro-drug that gets activated by cytochrome P450 (CYP)
 > 2B6 enzyme

 - It is a potent alkylating agent, causes intra and inter
 > cross-link breaks

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **Use:**

vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.md
vector_chunks.text:
ug that gets activated by cytochrome P450 (CYP)
 > 2B6 enzyme

 - It is a potent alkylating agent, causes intra and inter
 > cross-link breaks

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- **Use:**

 - [Currently this drug is only utilized in relapsed onsets or as
 > palliative care.]{.underline}
 > ([[https://ascopubs.org/doi/10.1200/JCO.2024.42.16_suppl.5517]{.underline}](https://ascopubs.org/doi/10.1200/JCO.2024.42.16_suppl.5517))

[PLATINUM-BASED CHEMOTHERAPIES]{.underline}

**Cisplatin** was the first platinum-based chemotherapy to be used in
ovarian cancer. ![](media/image3.png){width="2.4146576990376203in"
height="2.4146576990376203in"}![](media/image2.png){width="0.9042410323709537in"
height="0.44006342957130357in"}

- **Structure**

 - It has four "molecular limbs" surrounding a Pt atom

 - **Mechanism of action: Inter and intra-cross-strand
 > links**![](media/image4.png){width="1.3000732720909887in"
 > height="1.2165824584426947in"}

 - Two of those are Cl- and the other two are NH3

 - One chloride ion is displaced by water to give the aquo
 > complex, in a process termed aquation

 - The water molecule gets displaced by the N-heterocyclic
 > bases on DNA. Guanine preferentially binds

> ![](media/image2.png){width="2.2390332458442694in"
> height="1.1502744969378829in"}

**Carboplatin** acts similarly to cisplatin but with less side-effects
(no nephrotoxicity)

vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.md
vector_chunks.text:
> ![](media/image2.png){width="2.2390332458442694in"
> height="1.1502744969378829in"}

**Carboplatin** acts similarly to cisplatin but with less side-effects
(no nephrotoxicity)

- It has a bidentate dicarboxylate (CBDCA) ligand in place of the two
 > chloride ligands

In the 1980s Carboplatin tested to be effective in ovarian cancer
treatment

[TAXANES: PACLITAXEL]{.underline}

**Paclitaxel targets β-tubulin**. Paclitaxel-treated cells have defects
in mitotic spindle assembly, chromosome segregation, and cell division.
Chromosomes are unable to achieve a metaphase spindle configuration.
Prolonged activation of the mitotic checkpoint triggers apoptosis or
reversion to the G0-phase of the cell cycle without cell division. In
addition, taxane induces cancer cell death by activating the cleavage of
procaspases and proapoptotic markers.

[It limits the development of cancer cells by causing a stop in the cell
cycle phases, specifically at the G2/M phase]{.underline}

![](media/image1.png){width="4.300073272090988in"
height="3.010051399825022in"}

**Treatment combination:**

1996: A phase III clinical trial led by Dr. Robert J. C. T. at
Gynecologic Oncology Group (GOG) tested the combination of carboplatin +
paclitaxel in women with advanced ovarian cancer. The trial compared
this combination to older regimens that used cyclophosphamide and
cisplatin.

vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.md
vector_chunks.text:
the combination of carboplatin +
paclitaxel in women with advanced ovarian cancer. The trial compared
this combination to older regimens that used cyclophosphamide and
cisplatin.

- **Results**: The trial demonstrated a significant improvement in
 > survival rates for patients receiving the carboplatin-paclitaxel
 > combination. **Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall
 > survival (OS) were notably better** compared to the older
 > regimens. It showed a higher response rate (i.e., the percentage
 > of patients whose tumors shrank or disappeared).

- This trial was a game changer. It not only confirmed the efficacy of
 > the combination but also established carboplatin and paclitaxel as
 > the standard of care for first-line treatment of ovarian cancer,
 > including HGSC.

- By the early 2000s, clinical practice guidelines from major cancer
 > organizations such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network
 > (NCCN) and the European Society of Gynaecological Oncology (ESGO)
 > endorsed carboplatin and paclitaxel as the standard first-line
 > treatment for ovarian cancer, particularly for advanced stages

Over time, while **carboplatin-paclitaxel** remains the backbone of
treatment, **maintenance therapy** with newer agents like
**Bevacizumab** (anti-angiogenesis) and **PARP inhibitors** (for
patients with BRCA mutations) have been added to help extend remission

vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Background, framework and strategic goals/Chemotherapy usage in ovarian cancer.md
vector_chunks.text:
aintenance therapy** with newer agents like
**Bevacizumab** (anti-angiogenesis) and **PARP inhibitors** (for
patients with BRCA mutations) have been added to help extend remission
and improve survival.

**Chemotherapy resistance:**

Resistance can into primary and secondary:

1. primary is caused by tumor cells before therapy exposure

2. secondary is tumor adaptation to the treatment, eg high expression
 > of target proteins

Breast, ovarian, gastric, and uterine cancers become chemo-resistant
after being treated with chemotherapy for a long time, especially
paclitaxel.

vector_chunks.source_file: Management & planning/Writing/Cell_cycle_project_structure.md
vector_chunks.text:
**What is cell cycle?**

Cell cycle is the process through which a cell is able to effectively
divide

**Cell cycle and antitumor immunity**

**Senescence**

Multiple events in the microenvironment, as DNA damage or deprivation of
nutrients can induce senesgence. Senesence is a form of arrest in with
not only the cell division is shut down, but also triggers the SASP
(senescence-associated secretory phenotype), linked to secretion of
multiple characteristic proteins (such as chemokines, cytokines or
proteases). Depending on the biological context, senescence could limit
or support the tumor progression:

Within cancer cells

- SASP may facilitate the proliferative signalling of the surrounding
 > cancer cells

- Senescence may be just a transitory state, possibly associated with
 > therapy resistance. Since the treatments often target
 > proliferating cells, this form of dormancy might confer protective
 > effects to tumor cells.

CAFs (Cancer associated fibroblasts) can become senescent

**[How is senescence traced?]{.underline}**

> Traits associated in ovca with cancer cell senescence (with doxo
> treatment) are:

- expression of senescence-associated ß-galactosidase

- micronuclei

- G1-arrest

Many researchers target for SA b-galactosidase + p21 (p16INK4A)
expression as well as IL-6 and IL-1alpha
